entry.title: SAP EWM–PP Integration: PMR-Based Staging, Consumption, and Finished Goods Receipt
entry.content:
Abstract & Introduction:

In modern manufacturing environments, seamless integration between production planning and warehouse execution is essential for ensuring efficient material flow, inventory accuracy, and real-time operational visibility. SAP S/4HANA provides this capability through the integration of SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) and Production Planning (PP), enabling synchronized execution of production supply and warehouse processes.

This article presents an end-to-end overview of SAP EWM and PP integration, covering both system configuration and business process execution. The paper explains key integration components such as production supply areas (PSAs), production material requests (PMRs), staging methods, warehouse task creation, material consumption, production confirmations, and finished goods receipt processing. It also highlights the interaction between production orders and warehouse activities, demonstrating how materials are staged to production lines and how finished products are received and managed within EWM.

Objective is to provide both functional consultants and business users with a practical understanding of how SAP EWM and PP collaborate to support efficient and integrated manufacturing operations.

Production supply area(PSA):
A production supply area is the location where components are staged and made available for production consumption. Its purpose is to Supplies components to production and Acts as the destination for EWM staging.

Control Cycle:
A control cycle defines how a specific material is supplied from the warehouse to a specific PSA and controls the staging process for production.

Material + PSA + Plant -> Staging Method

SAP PP/EWM, Control Cycles are classified based on the staging method used to supply components to the PSA.

1. Pick Parts Staging: Components are staged for a specific production order and picked exactly according to the order requirement.
2. Release Order Parts Staging: Components are staged automatically when the production order is released.
3. Crate Parts Staging: Materials are replenished in the PSA based on minimum and maximum quantities instead of production order demand.

In SAP EWM Advanced Production Integration (API), the control cycle can be configured with EWM Staging. In this case, EWM controls the staging process through PMR (Production Material Request).

  1. Single-Order Staging: Materials are staged specifically for an individual production order.
  2. Cross-Order Staging: Materials are staged collectively for multiple production orders instead of one specific order.

Under this document, I am trying to make a scenario for a finish material # 1001590 and having three components.

#1 1001575 – EWM staging (Single order release)

#2 1001596 – Replenishment of Crate parts

#3 1001597 – Manual staging

Customization:

SAP S/4HANA

#1 Maintain Storage location(SLOC)

This is an organizational unit within a plant where materials are physically stored and inventory is managed. it belongs to exactly one plant and represents a physical or logical storage area. This is the lowest inventory management level in ERP/MM where stock quantities are maintained.

SPRO->Enterprise structure->Definition->Materials Management->Maintain Storage location

Created a SLOC called “AFS”, which is used in this document.

#2 Assign Warehouse number to Plant/Storage location

This configuration tells, whenever stock is posted to this Plant + Storage Location combination, the stock is physically managed in this EWM Warehouse Number.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Interfaces->Map storage locations from ERP system to EWM

 

#3 Define stock types

Stock Type defines the inventory status of a product in EWM. It controls what operations can be performed on the stock and how it is represented between ERP and EWM.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Goods Receipt Process->Configure availability group for put away->Configure stock type

 

#4 Storage type for Production supply area

In SAP EWM, a Storage Type for Production Supply Area (PSA) is the EWM storage type that represents the physical area where components are staged for production consumption. I am not showing other storage types but only showing PSA.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Master data->Define storage type

#5 Warehouse process type:

A Warehouse Process Type (WPT) controls how warehouse activities are executed in EWM. It determines the behavior of warehouse tasks such as putaway, picking, staging, internal movements, and production supply.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Cross process settings->Warehouse task->Define warehouse process type

WPT # 2010 is created for stock removal, especially for Staging process.

WPT # 3100 is created for replenishment, especially for crate parts.

#6 Determine warehouse process type:

This step is used to identify the warehouse process type depending upon the document type and Item type.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Cross process settings->Warehouse task->Determine warehouse process type

#7 Link SAP ERP order type to SAP EWM Document type:

This step link production order type to warehouse and document type.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Interfaces->ERP integration->Production->Map SAP ERP order type to SAP EWM Document type

#8 Link SAP ERP order type to SAP EWM Item type:

This step link component type with order type, warehouse, document type and item type.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Interfaces->ERP integration->Production->Map SAP ERP order type to SAP EWM item type

#9 Define Document types for production:

This is one of important step which assign action profile and other details to document type and category.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Goods Issue process->Production material request->Define document types for production

#10 Define item types for production:

This configuration step in SAP EWM that specifies the different item categories used in production integration documents, such as Production Material Requests (PMRs) and production supply processes. The item type controls how materials are processed, staged, consumed, and monitored during production activities

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Goods Issue process->Production material request->Define item types for production

#11 Allowed Item types for production

This setting in SAP EWM that specifies which production item types can be used within a particular production process or document. It ensures that only valid item categories are processed for staging, consumption, and goods receipt activities.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Goods Issue process->Production material request->Allowed item types for production

#12 Customization delivery:

SCM Extended warehouse management->Extended Warehouse Management->Cross process Settings ->Delivery warehouse-request->Check Customising

Document type “OPC” is having below configuration details.

#13 Assign Storage type to search sequence:

Assign Storage Type to Search Sequence is a configuration step in SAP EWM that determines the order in which the system searches storage types when locating stock for warehouse activities such as picking, staging, or replenishment.

Storage type “0011” wherein we have a component stock.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Goods Issue process->Specify storage type search sequence

#14 Storage type search sequence:

This configuration in SAP EWM that defines the order in which the system searches storage types to find stock for warehouse processes such as picking, staging, replenishment, and internal movements. EWM checks each storage type in the defined sequence until sufficient stock is found.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Goods Issue process->Determine Specify storage type search sequence for stock removal

#15 Activate Replenishment Strategies in Storage Types:

Activate Replenishment Strategies in Storage Types is a configuration setting in SAP EWM that enables automatic replenishment of stock within a storage type when inventory falls below a predefined threshold. This ensures that sufficient inventory is always available to support warehouse operations and production processes without manual intervention.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Internal warehouse processes->Replenishment control->Activate replenishment strategies in storage type

Master data:

#1 Map the EWM PSA to the ERP PSA(/SCWM/PSAMAP):

This step that links the Production Supply Area (PSA) defined in SAP EWM with the corresponding PSA in ERP/PP. This mapping enables seamless integration between production and warehouse processes, allowing EWM to stage components to the correct production area based on Production Material Requests (PMRs). It ensures that materials are delivered to the appropriate location for production consumption.

#2 Work Center (CR01):

A Work Center in SAP PP represents the physical location, machine, production line, or group of resources where manufacturing operations are performed. It is used in routings and production orders to define where an operation will be executed and to provide scheduling, capacity planning, and costing information. In SAP EWM production integration, the work center can also be linked to a Production Supply Area (PSA) to support component staging and consumption processes.

#3 Created BOM(CS01):

A Bill of Material (BOM) is a structured list of all raw materials, components, semi-finished products, and quantities required to manufacture a finished product. It defines what materials are needed for production and in what quantity. During production order creation, SAP uses the BOM to determine component requirements, generate reservations, and create Production Material Requests (PMRs) for staging in SAP EWM.

Material # 1001590 is finish product and having three components (1001575, 1001596, 1001597) and made below BOM.

#4 Component stock availability(/SCWM/MON):

Mentioned component stocks are available for staging and consumptions.

#5 Creation of Routing (CA01):

A Routing is a master data object in SAP PP that defines the sequence of operations required to manufacture a product. It specifies the work center, operation steps, processing times, and production activities needed to complete the manufacturing process. During production order creation, SAP uses the routing for scheduling, capacity planning, and determining where components staged from SAP EWM will be consumed.

Created routing for finish product # 1001590.

#6 Control Cycle (LPK1):

A Control Cycle is a master data object in SAP PP that defines how materials are supplied from the warehouse to a Production Supply Area (PSA) for manufacturing. For Material # 1001575, a control cycle was created to define how the material is supplied from the warehouse to the production area. The EWM Staging Indicator (5) specifies that the material will be supplied using Production Material Request (PMR)-based staging in SAP EWM. During production order creation, EWM automatically generates a PMR and stages the required components to the designated Production Supply Area (PSA) based on the control cycle settings.

 

 

Below control Cycle for material #1001596, defines how a material is supplied from the warehouse to the Production Supply Area (PSA). It links the material, work center/PSA, warehouse, and staging method used for production supply. Staging Indicator = 2 (Crate Part Staging). Crate Part Staging means EWM maintains a fixed number of containers (crates) in the PSA. The system monitors the quantity available in the PSA and automatically replenishes the crates when stock falls below the required level.

 

Below control cycle for material # 1001597, with Manual Staging, SAP EWM does not automatically create replenishment requests or stage materials to the PSA. The warehouse user must manually trigger the staging process and move the required quantity from the warehouse to the PSA bin.

 

#7 Assign Bin to Product/Entitled in PSA for EWM(/SCWM/PSASTAGE):

Assign Bin to Product/Entitled in PSA for EWM is a step that defines the specific PSA storage bin where a particular product will be staged for production. It links the product (and, if applicable, the entitled party) to a designated PSA bin, ensuring that SAP EWM delivers components to the correct production supply location.

#8 Product Master(/SCWM/MAT1):

Product master is created with stock removal control as “PICK”

End to End Transactions:

#1 Production order (CO01):

Below production order is created for finish material # 1001590. And all three components are assign to operation # 10.

#2 Staging for Single order release parts (CO02):

Control cycle for material # 1001575 was created as EWM staging and single order release.

#3 PMR display(/SCWM/MON):

After material staging step, below PMR was created for material # 1001575.

#4 Creation of staging task(/SCWM/STAGE):

Staging task was created for material # 1001575, because this material is linked with EWM staging.

 

#5 Warehouse task display(/SCWM/MON):

Warehouse task # 100068 created for material # 1001575 to move the stock(staging) from 0011(normal storage type) to 1005(PSA storage type) + specific bin # PSA1-1005-BIN1

#6 Replenishment for the crate part(/SCWM/REPL):

This replenishment task is created for material # 1001596, as control cycle for this material is created as Crate part staging.

Warehouse Task # 100069(/SCWM/MON):

#7 Manual staging(/SCWM/ADPROD):

Manual staging can be done by /SCWM/RFUI(Scanner) or /SCWM/ADPROD(GUI). As in this example, I am using /SCWM/ADPROD.

 

#8 Summarized Warehouse tasks(/SCWM/MON):

Below three warehouse tasks are created for all three components.

#9 Confirm Warehouse tasks(/SCWM/MON):

Warehouse task can be confirmed by /SCWM/RFUI(Scanner) or /SCWM/MON(GUI), however I am confirming using /SCWM/MON.

#10 Stock status in PSA bins (/SCWM/MON):

After warehouse tasks confirmation, stock is correctly populated in PSA bins.

#11 Consumption of components against Production order (/SCWM/RFUI):

First component # 1001575 was consumed using manufacturing order # 1001345.

#12 Consumption of components using CO15:

Remaining two components (#1001596, 1001597) are consumed using production order confirmation.

 

#13 Outbound delivery order((/SCWM/PRDO)

After production order confirmation, below outbound delivery order was created

#14 Outbound delivery(/SCWM/PRDO):

After post goods issue, below outbound delivery was created.

#15 Goods receipt of Finish material:

After Production order confirmation ERP Inbound delivery # 180109341 was created automatically and as a next step Inbound delivery # 325 was created in EWM automatically.

#16 Warehouse task(/SCWM/MON):

Below warehouse task was confirmed and now stock is available.

Conclusion:

The integration of SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) and Production Planning (PP) in SAP S/4HANA provides a robust framework for synchronizing warehouse and manufacturing operations. By connecting production orders with warehouse execution processes, organizations can ensure timely material availability, improve inventory accuracy, and enhance overall production efficiency.

Through key integration components such as Production Material Requests (PMRs), Production Supply Areas (PSAs), material staging, warehouse task processing, production confirmations, and finished goods receipt management, SAP enables a seamless flow of information and materials across the manufacturing environment. This integration eliminates manual interventions, increases process visibility, and supports real-time inventory updates throughout the production lifecycle.

By implementing the configuration and process steps described in this article, businesses can establish a tightly integrated production supply chain that supports efficient manufacturing execution, accurate inventory management, and enhanced warehouse productivity within SAP S/4HANA.

 Author: Naveen Chandra Sharma

(Naveen is SAP Solution architect having 29+ years of experience which includes 20+ years into SAP consulting with expertise in SAP ECC, SAP S/4HANA and integration with other external system like MES, CIMCORP etc. He worked on various type of project flavors like Implementation, Enhancement and support projects in various industries Manufacturing, Finance, Pharmaceutical, Food, Telecommunication)

entry.title: SAP EWM DODO — The 4 Variants You Must Know Before You Configure (DODO in Embedded S/4HANA 2025 EWM)
entry.content:
This blog is based on hands-on configuration and troubleshooting of DODO in an S/4HANA 2025 embedded EWM sandbox. All findings are from direct sandbox work — not copied from SAP Help. Screenshots are from warehouse EPG1 on S/4HANA 2025 FPS01.

Objective

The purpose of this blog is to present a practical framework for SAP EWM Direct Outbound Delivery Order (DODO) in embedded S/4HANA 2025. DODO allows creating an Outbound Delivery Order (ODO) directly in EWM without a preceding ERP delivery — EWM is the source, and S/4HANA creates the corresponding document dynamically.

Many consultants — myself included — have spent hours debugging DODO only to discover they were configuring the wrong variant entirely. This blog presents a clear 4-variant framework to help you choose the right path before you configure, and a diagnostic guide for the most common blocker: the IS_HEADER_ORG empty payload in SMQ2.

 

Background — What Is DODO?

In the standard SAP outbound flow, the sequence is: S/4HANA creates a sales order → creates an outbound delivery → distributes it to EWM → EWM executes the delivery. DODO reverses the first step: EWM creates the ODO first, and S/4HANA creates the corresponding ERP document dynamically via qRFC.

This is particularly useful in scenarios where the warehouse initiates the outbound process — for example, directly loading a production output pallet onto a shuttle truck to another plant, without waiting for an ERP-initiated delivery.

 

Section 1: The 4 DODO Variants

After researching 5 sources (SAP Help, two SAP Community blogs, an SAP Press project config guide, and direct sandbox work), I identified four distinct DODO variants in embedded S/4HANA 2025. Each has a different document type, ERP communication path, and function module called in the background.

Choosing the wrong variant is the #1 reason DODO implementations fail. The document type (OPIG vs ODSH), the ship-to partner type (customer BP vs plant BP), and the Comm. to ERP setting on the document type all determine which ERP path is followed. These are not interchangeable.

 

Variant

Doc Type

Item Type

Ship-To Partner

FM Called in Background

Use Case

1 — Customer Pickup

OPIG

ODPG

Customer BP (external)

/ SPE/OUTB_DELIVERY_SAVEREPLICA

External customer collects from warehouse without prior sales order

2 — Direct STO via OPIG

OPIG

ODPG

Plant BP (T001W.KUNNR)

/ SPE/CREATE_STO_FOR_DIRDLV + /SPE/DELIVERY_CREATE_FROM_STO

Plant-to-plant STO — SAP recommended path for embedded S/4HANA

3 — Direct STO via ODSH

ODSH

ODSH

Plant BP

/ SPE/CREATE_STO_FOR_DIRDLV + /SPE/DELIVERY_CREATE_FROM_STO

Plant-to-plant STO via ODSH doc type — requires specific Comm. to ERP setting

4 — Goods Movement Only

ODSH

ODSH

Not required

/ SCWM/ERP_GM_SYNC_UPD_S4 + /SPE/GOODSMVT_CREATE

Scrapping, cost center issue, internal consumption — no ERP delivery created

 

Let me walk through each variant in detail.

 

Variant 1: OPIG — Customer Pickup (Standard Customer Delivery)

This is the classic DODO scenario. The warehouse creates an ODO using document type OPIG (Outbound Delivery Order for Pickup) and the ship-to party is an external customer BP.

The key setting on OPIG that distinguishes it from ODSH is: Pickup = A (Mandatory). This flag tells EWM that someone is coming to collect — and when combined with a customer BP as ship-to, routes the replication to /SPE/OUTB_DELIVERY_SAVEREPLICA, which creates a standard LE outbound delivery in S/4HANA.

 

 

 

The system flow:

  • /SCWM/PRDO: Create ODO with OPIG/ODPG, ship-to = customer BP, product + WPT
  • EWM creates warehouse order and warehouse task for picking
  • qRFC message sent to S/4HANA: /SPE/OUTB_DELIVERY_SAVEREPLICA with IS_HEADER_ORG (Sales Org/DC/Div/ShPt)
  • S/4HANA creates LE outbound delivery (type LF) — visible in VL03N
  • Pick, confirm WT, post GI in EWM → movement type 601 in S/4HANA

 

Variant 2: OPIG — Direct STO (SAP Recommended Path for Plant-to-Plant Transfer)

This is the SAP-recommended path for EWM-triggered plant-to-plant stock transfers in embedded S/4HANA. It uses the same OPIG document type as Variant 1, but the ship-to party is a plant BP (the customer number assigned to the receiving plant in T001W.KUNNR) — not an external customer.

When EWM detects a plant BP as ship-to on an OPIG ODO (combined with the Pickup = Mandatory flag), it routes the replication via the STO function modules instead of the customer delivery FM. This is the architectural difference  not the document type, but the partner type.

Variant 2 is the path described in SAP's own blog 'Advanced Supply Chains with SAP EWM — Direct Loading of Finished Goods.' If you are implementing a plant-to-plant DODO in embedded S/4HANA 2025, start here.

 

The system flow:

  • /SCWM/PRDO: Create ODO with OPIG/ODPG, ship-to = receiving plant BP (T001W.KUNNR), assign HU at loading
  • EWM detects plant BP → routes to STO FMs
  • qRFC: /SPE/CREATE_STO_FOR_DIRDLV → creates STO Purchase Order (type UB) in S/4HANA
  • qRFC: /SPE/DELIVERY_CREATE_FROM_STO → creates NL outbound delivery linked to STO PO
  • NL delivery distributed back to EWM for execution
  • Post GI in EWM → movement type 641 (GI for STO with delivery) in S/4HANA
  • SPED output triggers automatic inbound delivery at receiving plant

 

Variant 3: ODSH — Direct STO via ODSH Document Type

Variant 3: ODSH — Direct STO via ODSH Document Type

This variant uses ODSH (ODO Direct Outbound Delivery) instead of OPIG for the STO flow. It requires the /SPE/DIRDLV_TYPE table to have an entry with Doc.Cat = J (Stock Transfer) for the ODSH delivery type.

S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 sandbox — confirmed working (June 2026):

Initial testing suggested this variant was blocked because the standard ODSH 'Comm. to ERP' field did not appear to expose an STO routing option, and the system was calling /SPE/OUTB_DELIVERY_SAVEREPLICA instead of /SPE/CREATE_STO_FOR_DIRDLV. Following feedback from Aleš Knor (SAP Community Explorer, running Variant 3 in production on S/4HANA 2023), the sandbox was retested with the /SPE/DIRDLV_TYPE entry correctly configured with Doc.Cat = J.

Result: STO and Outbound Delivery created successfully in S/4HANA 2025 via the same qRFC path as Variant 2. GI confirmed end-to-end. The earlier finding was a configuration gap, not a platform limitation.

The critical config prerequisite: the /SPE/DIRDLV_TYPE table must have a correctly configured entry for ODSH with Doc.Cat = J. Without this entry the system cannot route to /SPE/CREATE_STO_FOR_DIRDLV regardless of the Comm. to ERP setting on the document type.

Variant 3 vs Variant 2: both are now confirmed working in S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 for plant-to-plant DODO. Variant 2 (OPIG + plant BP) is SAP's documented recommended path. Variant 3 (ODSH) is a valid alternative where ODSH is already in use in the implementation.

Variant 4: ODSH — Goods Movement Only (No ERP Delivery)

This variant uses ODSH with ‘Comm. to ERP = B (Via Goods Movement Interface)’. No LE delivery or STO is created in S/4HANA — stock is moved via a direct goods movement posting. This is the intended path for scrapping, cost center issues, internal consumption, and VAS component reservations.

 

 

The system flow:

  • /SCWM/PRDO: Create ODO with ODSH, Comm. to ERP = B, product + WPT (no ship-to customer needed)
  • EWM creates WO + WT, pick and confirm
  • Post GI in EWM
  • Synchronous goods movement: /SCWM/ERP_GM_SYNC_UPD_S4 + /SPE/GOODSMVT_CREATE
  • Material document created in S/4HANA — no delivery — verify in MB51/MIGO

S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 sandbox observation: When creating a custom document type (ZDSH) by copying the standard ODSH, the Comm. to ERP field was greyed out and could not be changed to “B — Via Goods Movement Interface.” The root cause was not fully investigated in this session. The config path above documents the intended SAP design.

Variant 4 vs /SCWM/ADGI — when does ODSH add value?

A natural question: if /SCWM/ADGI already handles scrapping directly in EWM, why configure Variant 4 at all? For pure scrapping with no process control requirements, /SCWM/ADGI is the simpler and recommended path. However, ODSH-based Variant 4 provides architectural value in three specific scenarios: (1) Process control and workflow — ODSH creates a formal ODO with warehouse order and task, allowing a physical confirmation step before the goods movement. /SCWM/ADGI posts directly with no WT/WO. Where supervisor approval or quality hold release is required before scrapping, ODSH is the right design. (2) HU-managed stock with lifecycle requirements — where a sealed HU must be formally deactivated as part of the goods movement, the ODSH warehouse task flow handles HU lifecycle correctly. (3) Audit trail and document traceability — the ODO-to-material-document chain provides granular traceability that /SCWM/ADGI does not, which matters in pharma, food, and medical device implementations. If none of these apply, use /SCWM/ADGI.

 

Section 2: Key Configuration Steps

Below is a consolidated list of the key configuration steps required across Variants 1 and 2 (OPIG/ODPG path). Variant 4 requires only the ODSH document type change.

2.1 EWM: Define Document Type OPIG

IMG: SCM Extended Warehouse Management > EWM > Goods Issue Process > Outbound Delivery > Define Document Types for Outbound Delivery Process

Field

Value

Note

Document Type

OPIG

Standard SAP — do not copy unless required

Pickup

A — Mandatory

KEY FIELD — distinguishes OPIG from ODSH. Triggers STO routing when plant BP is ship-to.

Comm. to ERP

System Default

Do not change for Variants 1 and 2

Process Profile

/SCWM/OUT_PRD_PICKUP

Standard pickup profile

2.2 S/4HANA: Delivery Type Determination for DODO

Table: /SPE/DIRDLV_TYPE

Field

Value

Doc.Cat.

J (Stock Transfer)

EWM Del. Type

* (wildcard)

Initiator

* (wildcard)

Warehouse

Your warehouse number

Del. type

ODSH

IC DlvTy

NL (intra-company)

CC DlvTy

NLCC (cross-company)

2.3 S/4HANA: Item Category Determination

Table: /SPE/DIRITM_CATG

Field

Value

EWM Doc Type / Item Type

ODSH / ODSH

IC ReplItC

NLN (intra-company)

CC ReplItC

NLC (cross-company)

2.4 S/4HANA: STO Setup (Variant 2 specific)

IMG: MM > Purchasing > Purchase Order > Set Up Stock Transport Order

  • Document type: UB
  • Supplying plant: your sending plant
  • Delivery type: NL
  • Item category: NLN

2.5 Master Data: Receiving Plant as Customer

  • T001W: CustNoPlnt = plant customer number for receiving plant
  • T001W: SO ICB = sending plant sales org (critical for IS_HEADER_ORG derivation)
  • T001W: VSTEL = shipping point for sending plant. CRITICAL: this field is populated via SPRO → Logistics General → Handling Unit Management → Basics → Materials Management → Inventory Management → HU Requirement for Storage Locations and Default Values for Deliveries. Per SAP Note 2698417: OVXC does NOT populate T001W.VSTEL (OVXC is for shipping point determination in sales orders only). Transaction OMGN does not exist in S/4HANA. See Section 3 for full diagnostic detail — this is the most commonly missed field in all DODO implementations.
  • KNVV: Extend plant customer to sending plant sales area (VKORG/VTWEG/SPART)

 

Section 3: The Most Common Blocker — IS_HEADER_ORG Empty in SMQ2

If you have configured DODO and are getting error VP201 'Customer not maintained for sales area' in SMQ2, the first thing to check is NOT the customer master. Check the IS_HEADER_ORG parameter in the SMQ2 queue container display.

SMQ2 — Inbound Queue showing /SPE/OUTB_DELIVERY_SAVEREPLICA with VP201 error: “Customer 316 not maintained for sales area”

 

IS_HEADER_ORG is the import parameter of /SPE/OUTB_DELIVERY_SAVEREPLICA that contains the organizational unit data for the ERP delivery: Shipping Point (ShPt), Sales Org (SOrg), Distribution Channel (DC), Division (Div), and Warehouse Number (WhN).

When IS_HEADER_ORG is empty, the system cannot determine which sales area to use for the delivery, and VP201 fires regardless of whether the customer is correctly extended to a sales area. The customer master is not the problem — the derivation logic upstream is the problem.

Do not spend time extending customers to additional sales areas when IS_HEADER_ORG is blank. Fix the derivation first.

The Fix: How to Correctly Populate T001W.VSTEL

This is the most misunderstood step in DODO configuration. Two commonly referenced transactions do NOT work:

OMGN — does not exist in S/4HANA (ECC-era transaction)

OVXC — assigns shipping points for shipping point determination in sales orders. Per SAP Note 2698417, OVXC does NOT populate T001W.VSTEL. Many consultants waste hours here.

The correct path (confirmed in S/4HANA 2025 FPS01):

SPRO → Logistics General → Handling Unit Management →

Basics → Materials Management → Inventory Management →

HU Requirement for Storage Locations and Default Values for Deliveries

In this screen, select your supplying plant (PG11 in my sandbox) and assign the relevant shipping point (PGS1). This writes directly to T001W.VSTEL. Once saved, run /$SYNC, delete any stuck SMQ2 entry, create a fresh ODO — IS_HEADER_ORG will now populate with the shipping point.

IS_HEADER_ORG Root Cause Hierarchy (ranked by likelihood)

Priority

Root cause

Check

Fix

1 — MOST LIKELY

Shipping Point (VSTEL) blank for sending plant in T001W

SE16N > T001W > sending plant > field VSTEL

Assign shipping point via: SPRO > LE > Shipping > Shipping Points > Assign Shipping Points to Plants. Use TVSTZ table to find valid shipping points for the plant.

2

Sales Org not assigned to sending plant

SPRO > Enterprise Structure > Assignment > SD > Assign Sales Org - DC - Plant

Add entry for your sales org/DC → sending plant combination

3

Old SMQ2 queue entry — config changed after creation

Check FM name in SMQ2 queue container display — still /SPE/OUTB_DELIVERY_SAVEREPLICA?

Delete old queue entry + delete old ODO + /$SYNC + create fresh ODO. Queue entries are frozen at creation time — they replay original routing.

4

OPIG doc type missing sales area context

IS_HEADER_DATA.DlvTy in SMQ2 — what delivery type is the system trying to create?

Verify /SPE/DIRDLV_TYPE entry for your warehouse exists with correct Doc.Cat

 

Queue Container Display — IS_HEADER_ORG highlighted (yellow), right panel showing all fields empty: Delivery | ShPt | SOrg. | LoadPt | SOff. | WhN | SDst | Door | Unload.pt.

 

Section 4: EWM Warehouse Task Creation — Independent of ERP Replication

An important observation from sandbox testing: the EWM side of DODO (ODO creation, warehouse order, warehouse task, staging) works correctly even when the ERP replication is blocked in SMQ2.

This means you can validate and test all EWM-side DODO configuration (storage type, WPT determination, bin selection, warehouse task creation) before resolving the ERP replication blocker. The EWM and ERP sides can be debugged independently.

In my sandbox, Warehouse Order 3000003721 and Warehouse Task 1000006896 were successfully created against the ODO with:

  • Process Type: 2010 (Stock Removal)
  • Source Storage Type: DODO (staging area)
  • Source Bin: DD1
  • Destination Storage Type: Y920
  • Destination Bin: ST03 (staging bay for truck loading)
  • WO Creation Rule: DODO

 

Section 5: Configuration Summary Table

Config item

Variant 1

Variant 2

Variant 3

Variant 4

EWM Doc Type

OPIG

OPIG

ODSH

ODSH

Ship-to partner

Customer BP

Plant BP

Plant BP

Not required

OPIG Pickup setting

A — Mandatory

A — Mandatory

N/A

N/A

N/A

B — Via Goods Mvmt

/SPE/DIRDLV_TYPE

Doc.Cat=J required

Doc.Cat=J required

Doc.Cat=J required

Not required

Not required

Required

T001W.VSTEL (ShPt)

Required

Required

KNVV sales area extension

Required

Required for plant customer

Required for plant customer

Not required

FM called

OUTB_DELIVERY_SAVEREPLICA

CREATE_STO_FOR_DIRDLV

CREATE_STO_FOR_DIRDLV (if working)

ERP_GM_SYNC_UPD_S4

ERP document created

LE Outbound Delivery (LF)

STO PO (UB) + NL Delivery

STO PO (UB) + NL Delivery

Material Document only

S/4HANA 2025 status

Standard — confirmed

SAP recommended — confirmed

Blocked in standard ODSH

S/4HANA 2025 FPS01: Comm. to ERP field greyed out on copied ZDSH doc type — field lock root cause not investigated. /SCWM/ADGI recommended as practical alternative. Architectural value of ODSH documented for process control, HU lifecycle, and audit trail scenarios.

 

Conclusion

DODO is not a single process — it has four distinct variants, each with different document types, partner configurations, ERP communication paths, and function modules. Choosing the wrong variant before configuring is the single most common reason DODO implementations stall.

The key takeaways from this blog:

  • For plant-to-plant STO in embedded S/4HANA 2025: use Variant 2 (OPIG + plant BP). This is SAP's recommended path.
  • When IS_HEADER_ORG is empty in SMQ2: check T001W.VSTEL (Shipping Point) for the sending plant first — this is the most commonly missed field.
  • Always create a fresh ODO after SPRO config changes — old queue entries replay original routing regardless of what was changed.
  • EWM warehouse task creation is independent of ERP replication — validate EWM-side config before chasing the SMQ2 blocker.

For OPIG-based DODO (Variants 1 and 2): Picking status “Not Relevant” is by design — not a misconfiguration. GI must always be initiated from EWM (/SCWM/PRDO), never from VL02N in S/4HANA.

For internal goods movements without process control requirements: /SCWM/ADGI is simpler and sufficient. Use Variant 4 (ODSH) when you need a formal warehouse task confirmation step, HU lifecycle management, or a complete document audit trail — and first verify Comm. to ERP field availability in your system version.

 

I hope this blog saves you the hours of debugging it took me to arrive at this framework. Please leave a comment if you have encountered a DODO variant not covered here, if you have found a working path for Variant 3 (ODSH STO) in S/4HANA 2025, or if you have successfully configured Variant 4 with Comm. to ERP = B in S/4HANA 2025 — I would be keen to understand the field lock behaviour others have seen.

 

Happy Learning!

Yogesh Agarwal

Enterprise Logistics Execution Specialist | SAP EWM & TM | Warehouse Transformation | SAP MM/LE Consultant | MSCG | S/4HANA 2025 EWM

LinkedIn: linkedin.com/in/ayogesh.

entry.title: SAP EWM - Smart Way to Prevent Putaway for Delivery on Failed QRFC Queues
entry.content:
Business Scenario

In SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM), when we post a Goods Receipt (GR), any subsequent queue failure in SMQ1 (Outbound Queue) or SMQ2 (Inbound Queue) prevents the GR data from being communicated back to the ERP system.

Quite often, this goes unnoticed because users assume the system will process the GR in ERP without errors. As a result, warehouse tasks (WT) for the putaway are created (either manually or automatically) and subsequently confirmed. While this correctly updates the physical and logical stock in EWM, the ERP system remains un-updated, leading to critical inventory mismatches between the two systems.

The Challenge & Solution

I recently worked on a business requirement where the client faced significant operational challenges in identifying and resolving these queue errors. A major pain point was that once the putaway process is fully completed, reversing the transactions to fix the queue becomes highly complex or restricted.

To proactively resolve this issue, I implemented a custom enhancement during the Warehouse Task Creation stage. This enhancement automatically intercepts and aborts the WT creation if it detects a failed QRFC queue tied to that specific inbound delivery. Catching the error at this initial stage prevents the mismatched putaway from happening in the first place, drastically reducing inventory discrepancies and manual cleanup efforts.

Configuration: 
To make GR posting as mandatory first step and user should not directly go and create WT for delivery. Even if user try to create WT bypassing GR, Checkbox for WT after GR will ensure user to post GR first and then create WT.

Go to /SCWM/PRDI.  Select your delivery and post GR. Goods Receipt will be completed in EWM.  

In ERP, Goods movement status is still 'A' and a Queue is stuck in SMQ2 with an error. 

Now if we try to create a WT, for this EWM will not stop us and it can create WT to move product to final destination. 

We can save and confirm Warehouse order and Warehouse task. But since there is already a failed queue in SMQ2, communication will not happen to ERP and there will be inventory difference in two system. 

BADI /SCWM/EX_CORE_CR_ABORT can be implemented to add the additional logic for this business case. 

An implementation is created for BADI and activated. Below logic is added in a method ABORT which will be called during WT creation. 

Set a Breakpoint in a method and check while creating warehouse task. We can observe since the queue is stuck for a delivery in SMQ2, BADI is called during WT creation, and it is adding log + aborting Putaway warehouse task creation throwing error which will make aware user to resolve the queue error first. 

Putaway WT not created for delivery.

 

Conclusion: Using BADI /SCWM/EX_CORE_CR_ABORT we can abort WT creation for putaway if there is a failed queue. Above code can be structure and modified as per different scenarios and requirements and thus can be explored more.  

Note: All testings are performed on S4HANA 2023.

entry.title: Mass Print HU Labels frm the SAP EWM Warehouse Monitor-A Complete Techno-Functional Deployment Guide
entry.content:
⚠  Version & Release Disclaimer

This guide was developed and validated on SAP S/4HANA 2022 (FPS00) with Embedded EWM.

Field names, function module parameter names, internal table types, and BAdI method signatures can and do differ across EWM releases:

  •  S/4HANA 2020 Embedded EWM

  •  S/4HANA 2022 Embedded EWM  ←  this guide

  •  S/4HANA 2023 Embedded EWM

  •  S/4HANA 2025 Embedded EWM

  •  SCM-based (Decentralized) EWM 9.x

Before deploying any object from this guide in a different release, cross-check:

  1.  All TYPE references in DATA declarations against your system's data dictionary (SE11)

  2.  FM /SCWM/HU_READ_MULT parameter names and table types via SE37

  3.  BAdI /SCWM/EX_WME_CTRL method signature in SE18

  4.  Table /SAPAPO/MATKEY availability (used for material ID → material number resolution)

  5.  SSF_OPEN and SSF_CLOSE parameter lists — some parameters are version-specific

Introduction

In high-volume warehouse operations running on SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM), Handling Unit (HU) label printing is a routine but operationally significant task. The standard SAP approach requires operators to navigate to individual HUs and trigger label printing one at a time — a workflow that becomes a measurable productivity constraint at scale.

This blog documents the complete end-to-end deployment of a custom SAP EWM enhancement that extends the Warehouse Monitor (/SCWM/MON) to support Mass Print of HU labels — allowing operators to select multiple HUs simultaneously and generate all labels in a single spool job.

System: SAP S/4HANA 2022 Embedded EWM. The guide covers architecture, all ABAP objects, complete FM source code, SPRO functional monitor configuration, and 10 iterative issues encountered and resolved during development.

Business Requirement

Warehouse operators needed the ability to select multiple Handling Units simultaneously from the EWM Monitor and trigger label printing for all of them in one action. This reduces manual effort, eliminates repetitive navigation, and improves throughput during high-volume inbound and outbound processing.

Technical Architecture

Component

Technology

Purpose

 

SPRO: Define Object Class Methods

Monitor configuration IMG

Registers FM as HU object class action (Method Z00001)

 

SPRO: Define Methods Presentation

Monitor configuration IMG

Assigns action to SAP monitor, all warehouses, Seq 15

 

BAdI /SCWM/EX_WME_CTRL

EWM BAdI framework

Passes selected HU rows and warehouse to the FM

 

Print Dialog

ABAP Screen 100 (Modal Dialog)

User selects printer and SmartForm name

 

Print Controller

FM ZWM_HU_MASS_PRINT_MON

Orchestrates the full 12-step print loop

 

Label Template

SmartForm ZHU_LABEL_PNG

Defines HU label layout and content

 

Spool Output

SSF_OPEN / SSF_CLOSE

Creates single consolidated spool job for all HUs

 

Objects Created

 

Object Type

Object Name

Description

 

Function Group

ZWM_FG_MONITOR_HU

EWM HU Mass Print Monitor Function Group

 

Function Module

ZWM_HU_MASS_PRINT_MON

Main FM — orchestrates mass print loop

 

Include

LZWM_FG_MONITOR_HUTOP

Global data declarations

 

Include

LZWM_FG_MONITOR_HUI01

Screen PAI/PBO modules

 

Include

LZWM_FG_MONITOR_HUUXX

Function module source code

 

Screen 100

SAPLZWM_FG_MONITOR_HU

Printer and form selection dialog (Modal)

 

GUI Status

SCREEN_100

PF keys: PRINT and CANCEL

 

GUI Title

MASS_PRINT_TITLE

Title: Mass Print — Select Printer and Form

 

SmartForm

ZHU_LABEL_PNG

HU label output template

 

Test Program

ZTEST_HU_LABEL

Unit test for SmartForm standalone execution

 

Monitor Method

Z00001 (Obj. Class HU)

SPRO entry linking FM to HU object class

 

 

Step-by-Step Deployment

 

Step 1 — Create Function Group (SE37)

  • SE37 → Function Groups → Create Function Group
  • Function Group: ZWM_FG_MONITOR_HU
  • Description: EWM HU Mass Print Monitor Function Group
  • Package: $TMP for sandbox; assign transport package for productive deployment
  • Save and activate

 

Step 2 — Global Data Include (LZWM_FG_MONITOR_HUTOP)

Open in SE38. These declarations are shared across all modules in the function group.

DATA: gv_printer TYPE rspopname,

      gv_form    TYPE tdsfname,

      ok_code    TYPE sy-ucomm,

      gv_lgnum   TYPE /scwm/lgnum.

 

Step 3 — Create Screen 100 (SE51)

Program: SAPLZWM_FG_MONITOR_HU, Screen 100. Screen Type: Modal Dialog Box.

  • Input field: GV_PRINTER (length 30, label: Printer:)
  • Input field: GV_FORM (length 30, label: Form Name:)
  • Hidden OK field: OK_CODE
  • Pushbutton: PRINT (function code PRINT)
  • Pushbutton: CANCEL (function code CANCEL)

 

Step 4 — GUI Status and Title (SE41)

  • GUI Status SCREEN_100: assign function codes PRINT and CANCEL to F-keys
  • GUI Title MASS_PRINT_TITLE: title text = 'Mass Print - Select Printer and Form'

 

Step 5 — Screen PAI/PBO Modules (LZWM_FG_MONITOR_HUI01)

MODULE STATUS_0100 OUTPUT.

  SET PF-STATUS 'SCREEN_100'.

  SET TITLEBAR 'MASS_PRINT_TITLE'.

ENDMODULE.

MODULE USER_COMMAND_0100 INPUT.

  CASE ok_code.

    WHEN 'PRINT'.

      IF gv_printer IS INITIAL. RAISE PRINT_CANCELLED. ENDIF.

      LEAVE TO SCREEN 0.

    WHEN 'CANCEL'.

      CLEAR gv_printer. CLEAR gv_form.

      LEAVE TO SCREEN 0.

  ENDCASE.

ENDMODULE.

Step 6 — Function Module ZWM_HU_MASS_PRINT_MON (SE37) — Full Source Code

Import parameters: IV_LGNUM (TYPE /SCWM/LGNUM, pass by value), IT_DATA (TYPE STANDARD TABLE).

Exceptions: NO_HU_SELECTED, PRINT_CANCELLED, PRINTER_INVALID, SMARTFORM_ERROR.

 

⚠  Release note: Validate all TYPE references, FM parameter names, and table /SAPAPO/MATKEY against your specific EWM release before activating. S/4HANA 2020, 2023, and 2025 may differ.

 

FUNCTION ZWM_HU_MASS_PRINT_MON.

*"------------------------------------------------------

  DATA: lt_hu_hdr       TYPE /scwm/tt_huhdr_int,

        ls_hu_hdr       TYPE /scwm/s_huhdr_int,

        lt_hu_itm       TYPE /scwm/tt_huitm_int,

        lt_huident_loc  TYPE /scwm/tt_huident,

        ls_itm          TYPE /scwm/s_huitm_int,

        lv_matnr        TYPE char18,

        lv_maktx        TYPE char40,

        lv_quan         TYPE char20,

        lv_meins        TYPE char3,

        lv_batch        TYPE char10,

        lv_fm_name      TYPE rs38l_fnam,

        ls_ctrl_para    TYPE ssfctrlop,

        ls_print_options TYPE ssfcompop,

        lv_count        TYPE i.

  " Step 1: Validate input

  IF it_data IS INITIAL.

    RAISE NO_HU_SELECTED.

  ENDIF.

  " Step 2: Copy generic table into typed HU identifier table

  lt_huident_loc = it_data.

  " Step 3: Read HU details and items

  CALL FUNCTION '/SCWM/HU_READ_MULT'

    EXPORTING

      it_huident   = lt_huident_loc

      iv_lgnum     = iv_lgnum

    IMPORTING

      et_huhdr     = lt_hu_hdr

      et_huitm     = lt_hu_itm

    EXCEPTIONS

      hu_not_found   = 1

      internal_error = 2

      OTHERS         = 3.

  IF sy-subrc <> 0.

    MESSAGE ID sy-msgid TYPE sy-msgty NUMBER sy-msgno

            WITH sy-msgv1 sy-msgv2 sy-msgv3 sy-msgv4.

    RETURN.

  ENDIF.

  " Step 4: Show printer/form dialog

  CLEAR: gv_printer, gv_form, ok_code.

  CALL SCREEN 100 STARTING AT 10 5 ENDING AT 70 15.

  IF gv_printer IS INITIAL OR gv_form IS INITIAL.

    RAISE PRINT_CANCELLED.

  ENDIF.

  " Step 5: Get SmartForm FM name

  CALL FUNCTION 'SSF_FUNCTION_MODULE_NAME'

    EXPORTING  formname         = gv_form

    IMPORTING  fm_name          = lv_fm_name

    EXCEPTIONS no_form          = 1

               no_function_module = 2

               OTHERS           = 3.

  IF sy-subrc <> 0.

    RAISE SMARTFORM_ERROR.

  ENDIF.

  " Step 6: Sort HUs ascending

  SORT lt_hu_hdr BY huident ASCENDING.

  " Step 7: Set print options

  ls_print_options-tdprinter = gv_printer.

  ls_print_options-tddest    = gv_printer.

  ls_print_options-tdnoprev  = 'X'.

  ls_print_options-tdcopies  = 1.

  " Step 8: Set control parameters (set BEFORE SSF_OPEN - do NOT reset inside loop)

  ls_ctrl_para-no_dialog = 'X'.

  ls_ctrl_para-getotf    = ' '.

  ls_ctrl_para-preview   = ' '.

  ls_ctrl_para-langu     = sy-langu.    " Required - blank causes internal_error

  ls_ctrl_para-device    = 'PRINTER'.   " Required - missing causes spool error

  " Step 9: Open spool - ONCE before the loop (single consolidated spool job)

  CALL FUNCTION 'SSF_OPEN'

    EXPORTING

      output_options    = ls_print_options

      control_parameters = ls_ctrl_para

    EXCEPTIONS

      formatting_error = 1

      internal_error   = 2

      send_error       = 3

      user_canceled    = 4

      OTHERS           = 5.

  IF sy-subrc <> 0.

    RAISE SMARTFORM_ERROR.

  ENDIF.

  " Step 10: Print each HU

  LOOP AT lt_hu_hdr INTO ls_hu_hdr.

    CLEAR: ls_itm, lv_matnr, lv_maktx, lv_quan, lv_meins, lv_batch.

    READ TABLE lt_hu_itm INTO ls_itm

      WITH KEY guid_parent = ls_hu_hdr-guid_hu.

    IF sy-subrc = 0.

      " Resolve material number from EWM material ID (GUID)

      " NOTE: Field names and table /SAPAPO/MATKEY may differ in other EWM releases

      IF ls_itm-matid IS NOT INITIAL.

        SELECT SINGLE matnr INTO lv_matnr

          FROM /sapapo/matkey WHERE matid = ls_itm-matid.

        IF sy-subrc = 0 AND lv_matnr IS NOT INITIAL.

          SELECT SINGLE maktx INTO lv_maktx

            FROM makt WHERE matnr = lv_matnr AND spras = sy-langu.

        ENDIF.

      ENDIF.

      IF ls_itm-batchid IS NOT INITIAL.

        lv_batch = |{ ls_itm-batchid }|.

      ENDIF.

      WRITE ls_itm-quan TO lv_quan. CONDENSE lv_quan.

      lv_meins = ls_itm-meins.

    ENDIF.

    TRY.

      CALL FUNCTION lv_fm_name

        EXPORTING

          control_parameters = ls_ctrl_para

          output_options     = ls_print_options

          is_huhdr           = ls_hu_hdr

          iv_lgnum           = iv_lgnum

          iv_matnr           = lv_matnr

          iv_maktx           = lv_maktx

          iv_quan            = lv_quan

          iv_meins           = lv_meins

          iv_batch           = lv_batch

        EXCEPTIONS

          formatting_error   = 1

          internal_error     = 2

          send_error         = 3

          user_canceled      = 4

          OTHERS             = 5.

      " Suppress SSFCOMPOSER 150 (empty SmartForm - sandbox only)

      IF sy-subrc <> 0 AND

         NOT ( sy-msgid = 'SSFCOMPOSER' AND sy-msgno = '150' ).

        MESSAGE |Error: { sy-msgid } { sy-msgno } { sy-msgv1 }| TYPE 'W'.

      ENDIF.

    CATCH cx_root INTO DATA(lx_error).

      MESSAGE |SmartForm error: { lx_error->get_text( ) }| TYPE 'W'.

    ENDTRY.

  ENDLOOP.

  " Step 11: Close spool

  CALL FUNCTION 'SSF_CLOSE'

    EXCEPTIONS

      formatting_error = 1

      internal_error   = 2

      send_error       = 3

      OTHERS           = 4.

  " Step 12: Success message

  DESCRIBE TABLE lt_hu_hdr LINES lv_count.

  MESSAGE |{ lv_count } HU(s) sent to printer { gv_printer } successfully.| TYPE 'S'.

ENDFUNCTION.

Step 7 — SmartForm ZHU_LABEL_PNG (SMARTFORMS)

Import parameters: IV_LGNUM, IS_HUHDR (TYPE /SCWM/S_HUHDR_INT), IV_MAKTX, IV_MATNR, IV_MEINS, IV_BATCH, IV_QUAN.

Structure: %PAGE1 → MAIN window → HU_LABEL text node. Output fields: HU identifier, product code, description, quantity, unit, batch.

Important: Do NOT add barcode elements requiring GS1/business partner number ranges unless

the system is configured with a GS1 company prefix. This triggers SSFCOMPOSER error 150

or business partner runtime errors that are difficult to diagnose without this context.

Step 8 — Monitor Configuration (SPRO — No ABAP Required)

This step makes the action visible in the monitor. Without it, all ABAP objects are complete but the button will not appear for users.

SPRO path: SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Monitoring → Warehouse Management Monitor → Define Object Class Methods

SPRO navigation: Monitoring → Warehouse Management Monitor → Define Object Class Methods

8.1 — Define Object Class Methods

Field

Value

Obj. Class

HU

Method

Z00001

Method Funct. Module

ZWM_HU_MASS_PRINT_MON

MethodIcon

(printer icon — select from F4 help)

AutoRefrsh

Leave unchecked

Visibility

Always Visible

Text

Mass Print HU labels

Presentation Text

Mass Print HU

Define Methods: Obj. Class HU | Method Z00001 | FM ZWM_HU_MASS_PRINT_MON | Always Visible

8.2 — Define Methods Presentation

Click Define Methods Presentation in the Dialog Structure left panel. Create a new entry:

Field

Value

Warehouse (War...)

**** (four asterisks — all warehouses)

Monitor

SAP (standard SAP monitor)

Obj. Class

HU

Sequence

15

Method

Z00001

Text

Mass Print HU labels

Dialog

0

Mthd Pres.

3 — Accumulated methods menu button (right-click context menu)

Define Methods Presentation: **** warehouses | SAP monitor | HU object class | Seq 15 | Accumulated menu button

Configuration result:

  •  Action visible in right-click context menu on ALL HU nodes

  •  Standard SAP monitor (/SCWM/MON → SAP monitor)

  •  All warehouses (**** = system-wide)

  •  Sequence 15 — placed after standard SAP actions in the menu

  •  Type 3 (Accumulated methods menu button) = right-click context entry

8.3 — Runtime Result

After saving SPRO configuration, open /SCWM/MON, navigate to Documents → Handling Unit, right-click any HU row. The Mass Print HU labels option appears in the context menu.

Upon clicking Print Button, the message saying 5 labels are sent to printer is displayed as below:

Runtime: Mass Print HU labels in right-click context menu, standard SAP monitor, Handling Unit node

Step 9 — End-to-End Test Procedure

Step

Action / Expected Result

1 — Open Monitor

/SCWM/MON → SAP monitor → Documents → Handling Unit

2 — Select HUs

Select one or more HU rows using the checkbox column

3 — Right-click

Right-click on a selected row to open the context menu

4 — Select Mass Print

Click 'Mass Print HU labels'

5 — Enter Print Details

Enter printer name and form name ZHU_LABEL_CAG in the popup

6 — Confirm

Click Print button

7 — Verify Status Bar

'X HU(s) sent to printer [name] successfully'

8 — Verify Spool

SP01 — spool requests visible for each HU

Key Issues Resolved During Development (S/4HANA 2022 EWM)

The following issues were encountered on S/4HANA 2022 Embedded EWM. Some may not occur on other releases; others may present differently. In all cases the root cause required analysis rather than trial and error.

 

#

Issue

Root Cause

Fix Applied

1

RSPO_R_RSPOPNAME_CHECK dump

FM unavailable in S/4HANA 2022 EWM

Removed FM call; used IS INITIAL check

2

SSF_OPEN JOB_OUTPUT_INFO unknown

Parameter absent in this system version

Removed IMPORTING job_output_info

3

Control params reset in loop

ls_ctrl_para overwritten each iteration

Moved all settings before SSF_OPEN

4

Quantity showing long decimal

Numeric type formatted as full decimal

WRITE TO + CONDENSE

5

Internal error — language blank

ls_ctrl_para-langu not populated

ls_ctrl_para-langu = sy-langu

6

Spool error — device missing

ls_ctrl_para-device not set

ls_ctrl_para-device = 'PRINTER'

7

Business partner runtime error

Broken barcode node needing GS1 setup

Deleted node from SmartForm

8

SSFCOMPOSER 150 — empty form

No printable content nodes

Suppressed in exception handler

9

CX_SY_DYN_CALL_PARAM_MISSING

Dynamic FM missing exception wrapper

TRY/CATCH cx_root added

10

ENDLOOP missing after ENDTRY

Loop/try nesting structure error

ENDLOOP added in correct position

Key Technical Takeaways

  • The SPRO Monitor Configuration (Steps 8.1 and 8.2) is mandatory. The ABAP objects alone produce nothing visible. The FM must be registered against the object class and assigned to a monitor.
  • SSF_OPEN must be called once before the loop, not inside it. This creates one consolidated spool job rather than N separate jobs.
  • Dynamic SmartForm invocation via CALL FUNCTION lv_fm_name requires TRY/CATCH cx_root. Standard EXCEPTIONS clauses do not catch all runtime errors from dynamically resolved FM names.
  • Control parameters (ls_ctrl_para) must be fully populated — including langu and device — before SSF_OPEN. Both fields cause non-obvious runtime errors if blank.
  • Material ID to material number resolution in S/4HANA 2022 EWM uses /SAPAPO/MATKEY. Verify this table exists and is populated in your release before using this approach.
  • SmartForm barcode nodes referencing GS1 number ranges will fail at runtime in systems without a GS1 company prefix. Remove or disable in development and sandbox systems.

 

System & Release Information

Developed and validated on: SAP S/4HANA 2022 (FPS00), Embedded EWM

Cross-release applicability: The architecture and approach apply broadly to S/4HANA Embedded EWM.

However, field names, FM parameters, and internal table types must be verified in your target release.

SCM-based (Decentralized) EWM 9.x has different BAdI and SPRO paths.

Closing Note

Building this enhancement required working through standard ABAP patterns, EWM-specific APIs, functional IMG configuration, and several release-specific behaviors that are not documented in SAP Help. The corrections table and the version disclaimers above reflect the honest record of that process — included because that knowledge has direct practical value for anyone attempting something similar on S/4HANA EWM.

If you have built similar EWM Monitor extensions or encountered different behaviors on other releases, please share in the comments.

Stay Connected:

LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/ayogesh

YouTube: www.youtube.com/@YogeshSCMArchitect

Happy Learning!

entry.title: What’s New in SAP Signavio for Supply Chain Management: Selected Highlights for H1 2026
entry.content:
This post highlights selected SCM-related SAP Signavio analytics assets released over the last two quarters. It is intended to give supply chain practitioners a quick overview of what is new and where these assets can support process improvement.

Supply chain teams are under pressure to improve service levels, reduce cost, and build more resilient execution across warehousing, transportation, and planning. The challenge is no longer visibility alone. It is turning process transparency into targeted action.

This is where SAP Signavio Process Intelligence value accelerators can make an immediate difference. They provide ready-to-use analytics assets that help supply chain experts identify bottlenecks, reduce manual effort, improve data quality, and focus on the process changes that matter most for operational performance.

The latest supply chain content spans Planning, Warehousing, and Transportation across SAP ECC, SAP S/4HANA, SAP Extended Warehouse Management, and SAP Transportation Management.

Let’s start with Warehouse Management, where the newest assets address some of the most common operational bottlenecks.

 

Warehouse Management

For warehouse process excellence teams, the newest value accelerators focus on three priorities: improving outbound service levels, reducing internal operating cost, and simplifying inventory and system complexity.

Identify Improvement Potential for Embedded SAP Extended Warehouse Management (Outbound) 

This accelerator is a strong starting point for supply chain transformation teams that want to understand where outbound warehouse process execution is underperforming before diving into root-cause analysis. It provides a consolidated view of KPIs and process variants across embedded SAP Extended Warehouse Management, helping process owners identify where cycle times, handling effort, and rework are creating avoidable cost and delivery risk.

For supply chain experts, the value is speed. Instead of reviewing warehouse issues one by one, teams can quickly see which pain points deserve deeper analysis and where the greatest operational and financial impact can be achieved. This value accelerator package provides the transformation pipeline for additional such as “Improve Process Performance for SAP Extended Warehouse Management (Outbound)”.

 

 

Improve Process Performance for SAP Extended Warehouse Management (Outbound) 

If outbound warehouse process reliability is your priority in terms of OTIF performance or warehouse throughput, this value accelerator for helps translate execution data into targeted improvement action. It provides end-to-end visibility from sales order creation to goods issue and highlights the process variants, cross warehouse performance benchmarks, rework loops, exceptions, and delays that reduce on-time delivery performance. This value accelerator package requires to install the data pipeline from the value  accelerator package  "Identify Improvement Potential for Embedded SAP Extended Warehouse Management (Outbound)".

 

 

Improvement Opportunity Dashboard to Reduce Slow Moving Stock (EWM managed)

Inventory health remains one of the fastest ways to release working capital and improve warehouse space utilization. This dashboard helps to identify stock that has not moved within defined periods, including unrestricted-use stock, quality inspection stock, and storage bins without recent activity.

By turning dormant inventory patterns into actionable recommendations, the dashboard helps to reduce excess stock, free storage capacity, and focus on corrective action where it can improve both operational efficiency and balance-sheet performance.

 

 

Improvement Opportunity Dashboard to Reduce Warehouse Management Internal Cost 

Manual warehouse work is often a hidden cost driver. This dashboard brings transparency into cancellations, exception-driven processing, and manually assigned warehouse orders across inbound and outbound execution, making it easier to identify where standard processes are breaking down.

For operations leaders, that creates a practical path to higher automation and lower handling effort. By quantifying recurring exception patterns, this dashboard helps to prioritize fixes that stabilize execution and improve warehouse productivity over time.

 

Improvement Opportunity Dashboard to Remove Data Objects with no Transactional Usage in Supply Chain (SAP Extended Warehouse Management) 

System complexity is another common source of friction in SAP Extended Warehouse Management. This dashboard highlights customizing and master data with no transactional usage, such as warehouse task process types, storage types, empty storage bins, and warehouse order creation rules that may no longer serve a business purpose.

This gives supply chain and warehouse optimization teams a fact-based way to streamline their setup, remove redundant objects safely, and reduce the long-term maintenance burden of the warehouse landscape.

 

 

Additional EWM Process Flows Within the SAP Signavio Process Intelligence – Plug and Gain Approach

The Plug and Gain approach is especially relevant for transformation programs that need to harmonize process execution across multiple warehouses. It helps teams compare actual process execution against leading-practice templates, reduce process complexity, and accelerate standardization at scale.

Three EWM process flows are now supported:

  • Putaway warehouse task creation to confirmation (KXEWM00360)
  • Stock removal warehouse task (KXEWM00361)
  • Internal movement warehouse task creation to confirmation (KXEWM00362)

 

The following analysis perspectives are now supported for these process flows, illustrated here with the Putaway process:

Process Execution Analysis

Use this analysis to understand how the process actually runs, where execution deviates from expectation, and where data-backed improvement can support ERP transformation, automation, and continuous optimization.

 

Process Variant Analysis

Use this view to compare process variants, define a target state, and support process harmonization across sites once improvement measures have been agreed and implemented.

 

 

Further Resources

If you want to see the warehouse use cases in action, these SAP Signavio assets for EWM resources are a good place to start:

Self-guided Click Demo, through this Video Clip (8min) or in the Framework for Warehouse Transformations.

 

Transportation Management

In Transportation Management, the newest dashboards focus on reducing process overhead, improving data quality, and removing structural inefficiencies that inflate total transportation cost.

Improvement Opportunity Dashboard to Reduce Old and Obsolete Transactional Data in Supply Chain (SAP Transportation Management) 

Backlogs of old transactional documents are often a sign that standard closing processes are not working as intended. This dashboard helps to identify obsolete freight orders, freight units, bookings, transportation units, and settlement documents that continue to accumulate and create avoidable complexity.

 

That makes it easier to prioritize cleanup, reduce administrative overhead, and strengthen the reliability of transportation execution.

Improvement Opportunity Dashboard to Remove Data Objects With no Transactional Usage in Supply Chains (SAP Transportation Management) 

This accelerator focuses on unused customizing and master data. It reveals where freight agreements, location IDs, and document types are no longer actively used in transportation execution and may be increasing maintenance effort without creating business value.

For transportation experts, this supports a more disciplined cleanup strategy. Teams can retire redundant objects with greater confidence, simplify the system and process landscape, and create a cleaner data foundation for standardization and future optimization.

 

Further Resources

If you want to explore the transportation use cases in more detail, these resources are a useful next step:

Self-guided Click Demo ,  this Video Clip (8min),  or the related Framework for Transportation Management Transformations 

MRP

MRP-related analytical assets have also been expanded and further documented for practitioners.

Experience an end-to-end customer journey to optimize MRP processes in SAP ERPs with SAP Signavio Solutions in this Self-guided Click Demo , or through this Video Clip (6min).

 

Conclusion

Across these areas, the H1 2026 SAP Signavio content releases for SCM strengthen the journey from insight to execution with next-generation process intelligence capabilities and more flexible ways to capture transformation value. That makes these supply chain assets especially relevant for teams that want to combine fast diagnostics with a stronger continuous improvement discipline.

 

 

If you want to explore the broader SAP Signavio portfolio beyond the selected SCM highlights in this post, the following resources are a useful next step:

  • SAP Signavio May 2026 Release Highlights   
  • Process Analysis and Mining   
  • Process and Journey Modeling 
  • New AI capabilities and enhancements   
  • Process Transformation Management and Collaboration   
  • Process Governance

entry.title: Manyat Layout: Shifting Inventory Readiness Ahead of Wave Execution in SAP EWM
entry.content:
Manyat Layout: Shifting Inventory Readiness Ahead of Wave Execution in SAP EWM

 

Introduction

While studying SAP EWM Wave Management, I kept asking myself a simple operational question:

Why do we wait for Wave execution before preparing inventory?

In many warehouse environments, outbound demand is already visible before execution begins. Sales orders may already exist, shipping schedules may already be known, and expected inventory requirements may already be identified.

This raises an interesting question:

If tomorrow's outbound demand is already known today, can part of the inventory preparation begin before Wave execution starts?

This question became the foundation of what I call the Manyat Layout Concept.

The objective of this article is not to replace SAP EWM functionality. Instead, it explores whether physical inventory preparation can begin earlier when outbound demand is already known.

Standard SAP EWM Wave Process

In SAP EWM, Wave Management is configured through standard Wave Management functionality.

Common transactions and areas associated with Wave processing include:

  • /SCWM/WAVE

  • /SCWM/WAVETMP

  • Wave Templates

  • Wave Release Processing

  • Warehouse Task Creation

  • Warehouse Order Creation Rules (WOCR)

A traditional outbound process often follows this sequence:

Demand → Wave Creation → Warehouse Tasks → WOCR → Warehouse Orders → Picking → Goods Issue

This process is proven and widely used across warehouse operations.

Most optimization activities begin after the Wave is released.

Warehouse Tasks are generated.

WOCR organizes the work.

Warehouse Orders are created.

Resources begin execution.

The Manyat Layout Concept

Manyat Layout introduces an additional preparation stage before execution:

Possible Preparation Before Standard Wave Execution

Demand → Material Identification → Replenishment → Readiness Zone

Standard SAP EWM Flow Continues

Wave Release → Warehouse Tasks → WOCR → Warehouse Orders → Picking → Goods Issue

The philosophy behind the concept is simple:

Prepare First. Execute Later.

The objective is not to replace SAP EWM functionality.

The objective is to explore whether inventory associated with expected outbound demand can be prepared before execution activities begin.

If inventory is already positioned closer to outbound operations, the warehouse may begin execution from a more prepared state.

What Is the Readiness Zone?

The Readiness Zone is not a traditional storage area.

It is not intended to become a buffer stock location.

Instead, it represents a preparation area where inventory associated with known outbound demand can be positioned before execution begins.

Only materials linked to expected demand are moved.

The concept remains demand-driven rather than storage-driven.

The objective is operational readiness rather than inventory accumulation.

What About WOCR?

A common question is:

If WOCR already organizes work, why is this concept needed?

WOCR remains an important SAP EWM functionality.

The concept does not replace WOCR.

The concept does not remove WOCR.

Manyat Layout does not ask how to replace WOCR.

It asks whether part of the preparation effort can happen before WOCR starts working.

If inventory is already positioned closer to outbound execution:

  • Picker travel distance may decrease.

  • Search activities may be reduced.

  • Warehouse preparation may improve.

  • Execution may become faster.

WOCR can still perform grouping and optimization activities according to demand and warehouse requirements.

The goal is not to replace SAP logic.

The goal is to reduce unnecessary movement before execution begins.

Another Perspective: Execution-Stage Dependencies

Another perspective involves execution-stage dependencies.

In many warehouse environments, execution activities begin only after multiple planning and grouping steps have been completed.

If an issue occurs during these stages, warehouse execution may be delayed while corrective actions are performed.

When inventory has already been prepared and positioned in a Readiness Zone, the warehouse enters execution from a more prepared state.

The concept does not eliminate system issues.

However, it raises an interesting operational question:

Can early inventory preparation reduce the operational impact of execution-stage disruptions by improving warehouse readiness before execution begins?

This article does not claim a definitive answer.

Instead, it explores whether preparation itself can become an additional layer of operational readiness before standard SAP EWM execution processes begin.

A Real-World Example

Consider a warehouse that already knows the next morning's outbound requirements.

Under a traditional approach, inventory remains in its original storage locations until execution begins.

Under the Manyat Layout approach:

  • Required inventory is identified early.

  • Replenishment moves inventory into a Readiness Zone.

  • Inventory is prepared before execution starts.

  • Wave processing and WOCR continue as standard SAP EWM activities.

When execution begins, the warehouse starts from a prepared state rather than a waiting state.

Where AI Can Help

Artificial Intelligence introduces additional possibilities.

AI can support this concept by:

  • Analyzing historical demand patterns.

  • Identifying fast-moving materials.

  • Predicting future outbound demand.

  • Supporting replenishment decisions.

  • Recommending Readiness Zone preparation.

As forecasting becomes more accurate, warehouse preparation may become increasingly proactive rather than reactive.

The Real Question

This article does not attempt to replace SAP EWM standard functionality.

Instead, it raises a practical operational question:

If outbound demand is already known, can inventory preparation begin before Wave execution starts?

And if preparation begins earlier:

Can warehouse readiness improve before execution activities even begin?

Conclusion

Traditional warehouse optimization often focuses on execution.

Manyat Layout focuses on preparation.

The concept suggests that some of the most valuable warehouse improvements may occur before the first picking activity begins.

Fast picking creates activity.

Prepared picking creates efficiency.

And sometimes the biggest warehouse optimization happens before the first pick starts.

— Rahul Kumar

Manyat Thinking

X-Raying Warehouse Operations Through SAP EWM

entry.title: How to Determine a Custom Inbound Delivery Document Type in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP EWM integrates with SAP S/4HANA through delivery documents — the data objects that carry inbound and outbound logistics information across system boundaries. Every warehouse activity, from putaway and goods receipt posting to picking and shipping, is anchored to one of these documents.

This blog addresses a specific and frequently asked question in EWM implementations: how do you determine a custom inbound delivery document type in SAP S/4 Hana which could later be used to map with a custom EWM Inbound delivery doc type. The answer is not in standard Customizing — but it is a clean, upgrade-safe enhancement that any functional consultant can implement.

Business Problem

In SAP S/4HANA Embedded EWM, each confirmation control category has one inbound delivery type assigned — typically the SAP-standard type (such as EL), pre-configured with all profiles and BC Set activations that SAP ships out of the box.

The challenge: your warehouse needs to handle multiple inbound streams differently — standard PO receipts (NB), STO-based flows (UB), project stock, consignment, or third-party scenarios — each with different status sequences, incompleteness checks, partner roles, and quantity offsetting rules.

The instinctive response — modify the profiles on the existing standard delivery type — is exactly what must be avoided. That standard type is SAP's delivered baseline. Altering it risks breaking processes you never intended to touch, creates upgrade exposure, and contaminates a baseline other warehouse scenarios depend on.

Yet standard Customizing provides no condition logic to route different PO types to different inbound delivery types. You cannot differentiate processing per PO type, and you cannot safely modify the standard type that all streams share. This blog resolves that constraint.

For an example: Refer this blog to understand more about the problem statement:"https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-q-a/different-inbound-delivery-document-type-for-different-sto-doc-type/qaq-p/14060337"

Why a custom Inbound Delivery Document Type Matters for EWM

In SAP EWM, the delivery document type is the configuration anchor from which all warehouse processing behavior is derived. The following profiles are all assigned at the document type level — and each one directly impacts how deliveries are processed, validated, and completed:

 

Profile

What It Controls

Status Profile

GR-relevant statuses, putaway start/complete sequence, system status flow

Incompleteness Profile

Mandatory field enforcement — which fields must be populated before GR can be posted

Quantity Offsetting Profile

Open quantity calculation, over-delivery tolerance, partial GR handling

Date Profile

Planned GR date, actual arrival, slot booking — impacts yard management

Partner Profile

Which business partner roles (vendor, ship-from, carrier) are captured on the delivery

Reference Documents Profile

Allowed preceding document references (PO, ASN, STO) and document flow maintenance

Process Management Profile

PPF actions, exception handling, and process trigger behavior

Enhancement Steps:

Go to SE93, and type the transaction code ‘VL31N’, then click on Display:

Double click on the program below:

Using the command ‘Ctrl+F’, search for the include “MV50AFZ1’

Double click the include program:

Copy and paste the highlighted code (lines 53-57) in your box, save and activate:

NOTE:

  • SY-UNAME is the SAP username, so this enhancement triggers only for the deliveries created by the mentioned user ids.
  • EKKO-BSART is the Purchase Order document type, so a custom inbound delivery will be created only for PO with ‘NB’ doc type.
  • LIKP-LFART is the custom inbound delivery doc type.

 

Protecting SAP Standard Warehouse Configuration

Another key motivation for this approach — beyond enabling differentiated processing — is protecting the integrity of SAP-delivered warehouse configuration.

When S/4HANA with Embedded EWM is activated, SAP pre-configures standard Business Functions and BC Sets covering inbound document types, item types, status profiles, and all related mapping tables. These are structured for implementations to copy and extend — not to modify directly.

Forcing all inbound streams into a single standard EWM document type means any customization required for one process must be applied to the shared document type, affecting all streams that use it. The risks:

  • Upgrade risk — SAP may reset or adjust standard document type configurations during upgrades
  • Support complexity — troubleshooting becomes harder when custom and standard behaviours co-exist on one document type
  • Unintended side effects — changing a profile on a shared type affects every inbound stream using it

Best practice: Leave SAP-standard EWM document types and their profiles untouched. Create Z-prefix document types for all custom behaviors, copied from standard, with independent profiles assigned.

Upgrade Safety and Supportability

MV50AFZ1 is SAP's designated customer include for the VL31N delivery creation program, part of the modification-free enhancement concept. SAP explicitly reserves these includes for customer implementations.

  • The include survives Support Packages and S/4HANA upgrades intact
  • No modification flag is generated — the change is within SAP's provided extension framework
  • No re-activation is required after upgrades
  • The pattern of setting LIKP-LFART in this include is a recognized SAP customization technique

Always validate MV50AFZ1 against your specific S/4HANA release and follow your organization's enhancement governance: transport management, peer code review, and regression testing before production deployment.

End-to-End Configuration Summary

#

Where

Action

1

SE93 > VL31N > Program

Open include MV50AFZ1 from the VL31N delivery creation program

2

ABAP Editor (MV50AFZ1)

Insert EKKO-BSART condition logic; assign LIKP-LFART; save and activate

3

EWM SPRO > Goods Receipt Process

Define custom Z-prefix EWM inbound delivery document type

4

EWM SPRO > Inbound Doc Type

Assign status, incompleteness, qty offsetting, date, partner profiles

5

EWM SPRO > ERP Integration

Map custom ERP delivery type → custom EWM document type

6

EWM SPRO > Item Types

Configure item type mapping and process type determination

7

Testing & Validation

Create test POs per document type; verify EWM delivery type and profile assignment; test GR end-to-end

If you found this useful or have encountered similar scenarios in your EWM implementations, drop a comment below — I would be interested to hear how your projects have approached inbound delivery customization, as there could be several ways of achieving the same objective.

The enhancement was carried out on below version of the SAP S/4 Hana system (Embedded)

Happy Learning!

Thanks to read my blog - Yogesh Agarwal.

entry.title: The Missing Layer in Every SAP Go-Live: Why Hypercare Should Be Built Before Go-Live, Not After!
entry.content:
The fifth time I watched a warehouse operative stand frozen in front of a red error screen at 6 AM on go-live day — waiting for a support consultant who wasn't there — I stopped thinking about the symptom and started thinking about the cause.

I have lived this exact scene across five SAP EWM implementations — frozen food, automotive, pharmaceutical, building products, tires.
