Different industries, different warehouses, different error messages. The same scene every time. A worker who was trained three weeks ago, on a process that has slightly changed since, hits an error the training never covered. The shift supervisor doesn't know the answer. The consultant who configured that exact process rotated off the project last week, or is asleep in a different time zone, or is on another call. The truck is waiting at the dock. And the person who is supposed to keep goods moving is standing still, holding an RF gun, looking at a screen they don't understand.

This is hypercare. And after living it five times, I've concluded we've been solving it the wrong way.

Why Hypercare Keeps Failing — Structurally

The standard answer to a rough go-live is "throw more consultants at it." More floor support. Longer hypercare window. Round-the-clock coverage. I no longer believe that's the answer, because the failures aren't random — they're structural. Six causes show up on every project:

Consultant rotation. The person who configured your wave logic, your POSC/LOSC routing, your putaway strategy, your PPF output — they are not on the floor at 6 AM on day one. The knowledge that would resolve the error in thirty seconds has already walked out the door.

The 24/7 coverage gap. Warehouses run three shifts. Hypercare staffing rarely does. The night shift hits the same errors as the day shift, with even less support.

Accent and language barriers. A global implementation means the support consultant and the floor worker often don't share a first language. Under time pressure, on a noisy dock, that gap becomes a wall.

Knowledge walkout. When hypercare ends, the consultants leave — and every hard-won resolution they discovered leaves with them. The next project rediscovers the same errors from scratch.

The same errors, every project. Across five implementations I have watched teams independently rediscover the identical handful of go-live errors — a missing PPF condition, a queue stuck in /SCWM/MON, an exception code nobody assigned. We never built an institutional memory.

The screenshot barrier. When a worker can't describe an error in words, they photograph it. That photo gets forwarded to a consultant, who interprets it, who replies. The whole support loop already runs on screenshots — we just haven't built anything to receive them.

More Consultants Is Not the Answer

Look at those six causes together and a pattern emerges: this is not a headcount problem. It is a knowledge continuity and availability problem.

Adding more junior, rotating consultants doesn't fix continuity — it amplifies the churn. What's actually missing is something that holds the project-specific knowledge, is available on every shift, speaks the worker's language, accepts a screenshot, and never rotates off.

That isn't a person. It's a system — and it should be the floor's first-level support. Not a replacement for the consultant, but the layer that sits in front of them: it answers the 70-80% of go-live questions that are known, repetitive, and project-specific, and escalates only the genuinely new problems to a human. The consultant's scarce time stops being spent re-answering the same wave error twenty times a shift, and starts being spent on the issues that actually need an architect.

Here's the part that I think is genuinely new.

An Embedded AI Agent — Built in Parallel With Testing

The concept is an AI hypercare agent embedded in the implementation. Plenty of people have proposed an "AI chatbot for SAP support," so let me be precise about the design principles that make this different — because the difference is the whole point.

1. Built in parallel with testing — not after UAT. This is the headline. Every AI support tool I have seen is deployed after go-live, then slowly trained on real incidents while real people suffer. This agent is trained during the testing phase — every SIT defect, every UAT resolution, every project-specific configuration quirk feeds it as the project happens. It arrives at go-live already 90% ready, not as a blank slate that learns while the warehouse bleeds.

2. Solution-Architect-curated, weekly. A human quality gate sits in the knowledge pipeline. Each week during testing, the SA reviews what the agent has ingested and validates it. This is what stops the noise problem that makes generic chatbots useless — the agent learns verified resolutions, not every wrong guess from a test cycle.

3. Multimodal — screenshot first. The primary interface is the photograph of the error screen, because that is already how the floor communicates. The worker shows the agent what they see, exactly as they would show a consultant.

4. A non-negotiable contracted deliverable. This is a commercial reframing, not just a technical one — and I'll come back to it, because it's the part that makes the whole thing actually happen.

5. Self-learning with a validation gate. Resolved tickets feed back into the knowledge base — but only after a Solution Architect signs off, never automatically. This principle matters enough that I give it its own section below, because it is what separates this from a chatbot that confidently repeats wrong answers.

6. Built for the human factors. Accent-independent, anxiety-aware, available to a worker who was rotated onto this line yesterday. These aren't technical features. They're the reason the gap exists, and only someone who has watched it five times would design for them.

7. A controlled, always-current knowledge surface. Beyond resolving errors, the bot is trained on the project's own documentation library — OPLs, work instructions, process guides — and returns only the latest approved version of any document. It becomes the single place the floor can ask, in plain English, "what do I do when X happens?" and trust the answer is current.

The Bot Is a System Object — So It Gets Signed Off Like One

Here's a principle I'd argue is non-negotiable: on go-live day, this bot cannot be a surprise.

In a new SAP S/4HANA embedded EWM deployment, no warehouse process reaches production without passing through the full validation chain — unit test, SIT, UAT, regression, cutover sign-off — approved by the relevant process owners. A wave release strategy is signed off by the outbound lead. A production staging flow is signed off by the manufacturing and warehouse leads. An inbound GR process is signed off by the receiving lead.

The hypercare bot must be treated as exactly that kind of object. It moves through the same gates as every warehouse process:

  • SIT — the bot's resolutions are validated against the same test scripts the processes are validated against. If a resolution it gives would fail the SIT script, it doesn't ship.
  • UAT — the actual business users — the shipping clerk, the production supervisor, the receiving specialist — interact with the bot during UAT, not for the first time on go-live morning. They sign off that its answers match how they will actually work.
  • Regression and cutover — the bot's knowledge base is version-controlled and frozen at cutover alongside the configuration, with the same change-control discipline.

And critically: the bot is trained and socialized by the Organizational Change Management (OCM) team, not dropped in by IT. The same OCM workstream that runs the training, builds the work instructions, and manages the floor's readiness is the workstream that introduces the bot. So when the shipping clerk, the production supervisor, or the inbound receiving specialist opens it at 5 AM on day one, it is a familiar tool they were trained on and helped shape during UAT — not a random, fancy, surprise bot that nobody trusts. Trust is the entire game in hypercare. A tool the floor met for the first time on go-live day has zero trust. A tool they helped validate for six weeks has earned it.

What It Actually Looks Like — Four Real Scenarios

Let me make it concrete across the warehouse flows, in an embedded S/4HANA 2025 EWM deployment. These are representative of the exact class of issues that consume hypercare on every project — and each one shows a different capability the bot needs.

Outbound — the shipping coordinator and the "stock is there, but it isn't" problem. The coordinator runs the wave simulator in the shipping cockpit and it fails — the wave won't simulate. They check the warehouse monitor and the stock shows as F2, unrestricted-use in the warehouse, so by every signal on their screen the stock is available. The real cause is upstream: the batch is restricted because the QM team never released it, and the standard simulator doesn't surface that as the reason it failed. This is a genuine product gap — the simulator tells you it failed, not that a QM batch status is the cause. The bot closes that gap. The coordinator describes the failure in plain English; the bot, trained on this project's QM-EWM batch-release interplay, immediately points to the batch's QM status as the thing to check rather than the warehouse stock type — turning a forty-minute "but the stock is right there" investigation into a thirty-second answer. The same finding is also logged as a candidate enhancement to the simulator itself.

Outbound — the one-off scenario and the right version of the right document. A truck comes back to the yard because it was overloaded — a rare exception the coordinator handles maybe twice a year and has no memorized procedure for. There is an OPL for it, sitting in a shared drive among hundreds of other documents, and quite possibly in three slightly different versions. The bot was trained to reference that document library and tested to return only the latest approved version of any requested procedure. The coordinator simply asks, in plain English, what to do when a loaded truck returns — and gets the current OPL, not a superseded one, without hunting through folders or pinging a consultant to find out which version is live. This is the bot as a controlled, always-current knowledge surface over documentation that already exists but nobody can find under pressure.

Inbound — the receiving specialist who packed it wrong and needs to start over. The specialist realizes they packed an inbound delivery incorrectly on the first pass and needs to repack. The bot walks them through the recovery step by step: delete the existing handling units, use the Change Inbound Deliveries Fiori app to repack cleanly, then collect the new HU labels that print automatically from the assigned printer. This is the bot as a patient, step-by-step floor guide for a correction the specialist was never specifically trained on. And here is the important part — the bot knows the boundary of its own competence. If the new label does not come out of the Zebra printer automatically, and the bot was not designed or tested to troubleshoot printer output, it does not guess. It raises a ticket at the appropriate priority, assigns it to the right team, and tells the specialist it has done so. The specialist isn't left re-framing the same question to a hypercare consultant three times — the escalation happens cleanly and the resolution pace improves.

Production — the supervisor whose stock won't appear in MES. The production supervisor cannot see stock in their MES that should have synced from the EWM monitor. This is one of the most common and most painful go-live issues in a manufacturing landscape — an integration failure across the ERP–MES–EWM chain, where a posting in one system doesn't propagate to the next. The bot is trained to do exactly what a consultant would do to triage it: it takes a screenshot and/or the relevant data, optionally a line or two of context, and works through the integration checkpoints — did the material document post, did the EWM stock update, did the message reach MES — to isolate where the chain broke. If it can resolve it, it does. If it can't fully resolve it, the request routes to a real person already engaged on the project, with the triage already done.

The Bot Learns Only When a Trusted Human Signs Off

Those last two scenarios carry the most important design principle in the whole concept, so I want to state it plainly.

When a consultant resolves an escalated ticket, they cannot simply comment "issue fixed" and close it. That is how knowledge gets lost today. For the bot, a resolution only enters its knowledge base when a Solution Architect — or a peer of equivalent standing and trajectory — signs off that the resolution is correct, complete, and safe for the bot to repeat. Then the bot is retested against it, exactly as a configuration change would be retested.

This is the validation gate that separates this concept from a chatbot that confidently amplifies wrong answers. The bot relearns continuously — but only verified, signed-off knowledge, never an unreviewed "fixed" comment. A wrong resolution learned at machine speed and repeated to every shift is far more dangerous than no resolution at all. The human sign-off is not bureaucracy. It is the safety mechanism that makes self-learning trustworthy.

In all four scenarios: the error never had to be described in a second language, the consultant who built the config didn't have to be awake, the boundary of the bot's competence was respected, and every genuinely new resolution was captured — under human sign-off — for the next worker who hits it.

On the Architecture — Briefly

I'm keeping the stack high-level on purpose, because the originality here is when you build the agent and who curates it, not the technology. At a glance: a vision-capable LLM layer for the screenshot interpretation; a vector knowledge store trained on the project's own SIT/UAT defects, resolutions, and configuration documentation; integration into the implementation's ticketing system (the standard enterprise ITSM tool) so resolved incidents feed back through the SA validation gate; and a floor-friendly channel the workers already use. In an embedded EWM landscape it reads the same error and application-log surfaces the consultants already read — the difference is that it reads them at 5 AM, in every language, for every shift, and never forgets a resolution. The architecture is the easy part. The discipline of building and validating it alongside the warehouse processes is the hard part — and the valuable one.

Make It Non-Negotiable

Here's why this stays a concept unless the commercial model changes: as long as an AI hypercare agent is an optional add-on, it gets value-engineered out of the SOW on every budget-constrained project — which is all of them.

So don't make it optional. Price it into the implementation as a contracted hypercare deliverable, the same way you'd price the cutover plan or the training material. The economics are not subtle: the build cost of the agent is less than two weeks of a single floor consultant's hypercare time. One avoided week of go-live chaos pays for it. The argument to the client isn't "buy our AI" — it's "your hypercare will cost less and work better, and here's the line item."

That single reframing — from add-on to contracted deliverable — is what turns this from a nice idea into something that ships.

The Tool Does Not Yet Exist. That Is the Point.

I want to be clear and honest: I have not built this. What I have is five go-lives of watching the same preventable failure, a design that addresses each of its structural causes, and the conviction that the field is about to need it.

Every meaningful innovation in SAP consulting started as a named concept before it became a product. So I'm naming this one. An AI hypercare agent — built in parallel with testing, SA-curated, signed off through SIT and UAT like any warehouse process, socialized by OCM so the floor already trusts it, screenshot-first, and contracted as a deliverable — is, as far as I can find across SAP Community, SAP's roadmap, and the major SI tooling, not something anyone has yet put together this way.

I'd genuinely like to be challenged on that. If you've seen this built, tell me where — I want to learn from it. If you've lived the 6 AM frozen-screen scene too, tell me what you would add to the design. And if you're a Solution Architect or a delivery lead reading this before your next go-live: ask whether your hypercare plan is a stack of rotating consultants and hope, or something that actually holds the knowledge and stands in front of your floor as first-level support from hour one.

The scene I described plays out on warehouse floors every single week. We have the tools to end it. We've just been building them at the wrong time.

Yogesh Agarwal is an Enterprise Logistics Execution Specialist with 15+ years across SAP EWM and TM implementations in food, automotive, pharmaceutical, building products, and industrial sectors. He writes about SAP supply chain execution on SAP Community and LinkedIn.

entry.title: Decentralized Extended Warehouse Management and Quality Management in Two-Tier ERP
entry.content:
The SAP Community blog by @Sahil_Grover introduces a hybrid integration between decentralized SAP EWM and SAP Cloud ERP.

  • SAP EWM Integration - Inbound Processing from Supplier & Outbound Processing to Customer

The SAP Extended Warehouse Management Integration with SAP Cloud ERP - Part 1

  • Setup for The SAP Extended Warehouse Management Integration with SAP Cloud ERP

The SAP Extended Warehouse Management Integration with SAP Cloud ERP - Part 2

In this blog, we introduce a new scenario for SAP Decentralized EWM integration with Quality Management, where Quality Inspection is executed directly in EWM using the Quality Inspection Engine (QIE).

This approach is especially relevant for businesses that want to perform inspection decisions closer to warehouse execution, reduce dependency on ERP-based inspection activities, and enable faster Inbound processing.

Business Scenario Overview

In this scenario:

  • A Purchase order and Inbound delivery are created in SAP Cloud ERP
  • The Inbound delivery is distributed to the Decentralized EWM
  • Goods receipt is posted in EWM
  • Quality inspection is triggered and executed directly in EWM
  • Inspection results and follow-up actions are performed within EWM
  • Stock status is updated and synchronized with SAP Cloud ERP

The key differentiator here is that inspection happens exactly where the goods are physically handled — in the warehouse.

Key Configuration Focus Areas

To enable this scenario successfully, some important setup areas need attention.

  1. Quality Inspection Engine (QIE) in EWM

The Inspection object type, Inspection rules, and decision logic need to be configured correctly so that Inspection documents are created and processed as expected.

       2. Product and Warehouse Master Data

The warehouse product master and material setup must support inspection-relevant processing. Stock determination and warehouse execution behavior should align with the quality process.

       3. Delivery Integration Between SAP Cloud ERP and EWM

Inbound delivery distribution must work correctly so that the warehouse receives the required execution documents in time.

       4. Follow-Up Action Determination

Accepted, Rejected, and Exception outcomes should be mapped carefully to the right stock types and warehouse actions.

       5. Warehouse Process Integration

Inspection may be required at goods receipt, before putaway, at handling unit level, or in work center-related steps depending on the business design.

A well-designed configuration ensures that warehouse execution remains smooth and the quality process behaves consistently.

Process Flow

  1. Purchase Order and Inbound Delivery in SAP Cloud ERP

The process begins in ERP with procurement. Once the inbound delivery is created, it is distributed to the decentralized EWM system for warehouse execution.

       2. Goods Receipt in in Decentralized EWM

When the goods arrive physically, the warehouse posts goods receipt in EWM. At this point, inspection-relevant checks are triggered.

        3. Inspection Document Creation via QIE

Based on configured rules, EWM creates an inspection document using the Quality Inspection Engine. This document becomes the operational object for the warehouse-level inspection.

        4. Inspection Execution in EWM

The inspection is performed directly in EWM by the warehouse or quality user. This may include visual checks, packaging verification, quantity checks, damage checks, or handling-unit-level validation.

The warehouse user records the inspection result in EWM and completes the inspection process without switching to ERP for operational execution.

This is particularly useful in decentralized warehouse scenarios where speed and execution simplicity are critical.

       5. Usage Decision / Inspection Outcome in EWM

After inspection execution, the user records the final inspection outcome in EWM.

Typical outcomes include: Accepted, Rejected, Partially accepted, Rework required, Return to vendor, and Scrap

        6. Follow-Up Action in EWM

After the inspection result is recorded, EWM automatically determines the next action. Stock can be released to Unrestricted use, Blocked, routed for rework, returned to Supplier, or scrapped based on the configured decision logic

Conclusion

The 7L1-based Two-Tier Decentralized EWM scenario with EWM-based Quality Inspection is a strong option for businesses that want inspection to happen closer to warehouse execution.

By using QIE in EWM, organizations can inspect inbound goods directly in the warehouse, record decisions immediately, and trigger the right follow-up action without delaying operational flow.

In a Two-Tier SAP architecture, this approach provides a practical balance between central ERP control and local warehouse execution efficiency.

entry.title: Introducing Support Content — the additional Customer Support knowledge repository
entry.content:
Support Content is a curated knowledge hub that explains SAP systems from the perspective of Customer Support experts. Built from real Customer Support experience, it documents product logic, troubleshooting approaches, and recommended practices — helping you resolve issues faster and understand system behavior.

What you'll find there

Support Content provides practical information about SAP products and scenarios, including:

  • Explanations of system logic and expected behavior
  • Business process guidance and context
  • Step‑by‑step troubleshooting guides
  • Common configuration scenarios
  • Frequently asked questions (FAQs)
  • White papers and architecture examples
  • Best practices and recommendations

Content covers a broad range of SAP offerings — from newer topics such as SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP HANA Smart Data Integration to long‑standing products such as SAP Crystal Reports, SAP Datasphere, and SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) — and everything that falls under Customer Support’s scope.

How it’s used and maintained

Support Content is designed to be a practical reference for customers. Entries are linked to other SAP repositories and are frequently referenced to speed diagnosis and resolution. The repository is reviewed and updated over time, with new topics added according to demand and evolving product landscapes.

How can you share feedback?

Feedback is welcome and helps the repository stay accurate and useful. Use the platform’s comments feature on each page to:

  • Report unclear or out‑of‑date information
  • Suggest additional scenarios or examples
  • Reference related support cases or product versions for context

You have two options to help us to improve the content:

  • Feedback (anonymous): using the Feedback feature on the right-hand side of the page, where you can tell us how helpful the content is and add an optional text.
  • Comment (need an authenticated user): you can provide your comments and be specific on which piece of the text you are referring to.
    First, select the words and click on the button:

    Then, you can add your comment:

* The Comment option will be gradually introduced on the topics. 

When commenting, include a brief description of the issue or the suggested change to help reviewers act more quickly.

Why use Support Content

  • Saves time by providing authoritative explanations and troubleshooting steps
  • Helps you understand root causes and expected system behavior
  • Complements other SAP knowledge sources by focusing on practical, support‑driven guidance

 Explore the Support Content to find product guidance, scenario examples, troubleshooting resources, and much more, tailored to help with your support needs and make the most of your SAP landscape.

Want to know where Support Content originates? Look at this blog post.

Cheers!!
Marcelo Pinheiro
Support Content Business Owner

entry.title: Choosing Between SAP Extended Warehouse Management and SAP Logistics Management
entry.content:
SAP's warehouse management portfolio now offers three distinct paths: SAP Extended Warehouse Management for Private Cloud, SAP Extended Warehouse Management for Public Cloud, and the newer warehouse management solution released officially for customers in March 2026: SAP Logistics Management

In this blog, I would like to help customers select the best path between SAP Extended Warehouse Management for Private Cloud and SAP Logistics Management, based on their intralogistics profile.

What Is SAP Logistics Management?

Before comparing the solutions, let me address the most frequent question I hear from customers: "What is SAP Logistics Management?"

In a nutshell, SAP Logistics Management is a cloud-based logistics solution that combines warehouse management, transportation management capabilities, and Business Network for Logistics to optimize operations through network-centric collaboration. As a new SaaS offering, it unites warehousing and transportation dispatching, helping businesses optimize their logistics fulfillment operations and improve overall efficiency.

Setting the Foundation

This high-level definition serves as an initial foundation for any customer planning to implement an SAP warehouse management solution. The natural next question within the organization is: Which solution best fits my operations?

Both solutions serve distinct warehouse business models, and this statement should guide you on solution evaluation and due to this I would like to provide clarity and help customers avoid common mistakes when deciding between SAP Extended Warehouse Management for Private Cloud (Embedded or Decentralized) and SAP Logistics Management.

Core Principle

Before starting any discussion, it is essential to understand one key point:

SAP Logistics Management does not replace SAP Extended Warehouse Management for Private Cloud.

Instead, SAP Logistics Management extends SAP's warehouse portfolio, enabling organizations to optimize every site—not just large and complex distribution centers.

These two solutions serve different intralogistics profiles, with a deliberate overlap zone where either option may be appropriate depending on specific business needs.

The diagram below shows, at a high level, which solution is recommended based on the intralogistics profile. In the overlap zone (highlighted in yellow), both solutions might be applicable. Let us take a deeper look at the different features and scenarios that can guide you toward the right decision

IMPORTANT: Unlike SAP Extended Warehouse Management for Private Cloud—where new features or enhancements are released with each Feature Package Stack (FPS) or new SAP S/4HANA version—SAP Logistics Management delivers new features and enhancements on a bi-weekly basis. Visit  What’s New Viewer regularly to stay up to date with the latest content.

When to Choose SAP Logistics Management

SAP Logistics Management is best suited for organizations with low-to-mid complexity warehouses. It is designed for smaller warehousing and shipping facilities, scaling businesses, and second-tier operations of large enterprises. It seamlessly integrates with your core SAP S/4HANA Cloud (ERP) order processing and your network of logistics service providers and carriers.

Typical Characteristics

  • Full transparency into logistics operations
  • Scalable growth
  • Ease of access to carrier networks
  • Low-to-medium operational complexity
  • Need for rapid deployment across multiple sites
  • Focus on guided execution rather than deep optimization
  • Desire to eliminate paper-based processes and digitize operations
  • Limited or no advanced automation or robotics
  • Strong emphasis on speed of implementation and lower TCO

Common Warehouse Types

  • Local or regional replenishment centers
  • E-commerce micro-fulfillment centers
  • Production-support warehouses
  • Service and MRO warehouses for internal consumption

When to Choose SAP Extended Warehouse Management

SAP Extended Warehouse Management is designed for highly complex, high-volume warehouse operations requiring advanced optimization and automation.

Typical Characteristics

  • High throughput and performance requirements
  • Peak season processing with large volume spikes
  • Very high task volumes (thousands per hour)
  • Advanced wave management with multiple warehouse requests
  • Intensive use of transport units
  • Integration with warehouse automation technologies

Supported Automation

  • Conveyor systems
  • AS/RS (Automated Storage and Retrieval Systems)
  • AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles)
  • AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots)
  • Material Flow System (MFS)
  • Voice-directed picking

Advanced Capabilities

  • Complex process models (POSC/LOSC)
  • Cross-docking and merchandise flow-through
  • Yard management integration
  • Slotting and rearrangement
  • Task interleaving
  • Labor management and demand planning

The Overlap Zone: When Both Could Fit

Some warehouses fall into a gray area where either SAP Logistics Management or SAP Extended Warehouse Management could be appropriate. In these cases, a deeper evaluation is required.

Choose SAP Logistics Management if:

  • You want a unified platform combining Transportation, Warehouse Management, and Business Network
  • You prefer a modular, low-complexity solution
  • You need a simple execution layer
  • Automation is limited or moderate
  • You prioritize cloud-native architecture
  • You aim for reduced total cost of ownership (TCO)
  • You require a global template with fast rollout capabilities

Choose SAP Extended Warehouse Management if:

  • Your strategy is evolving toward advanced automation
  • You operate in a complex IT landscape integrating multiple ERP systems
  • Advanced routing and picking optimization are must-have capabilities
  • Your warehouse is expected to scale into a high-throughput environment
  • You handle complex exception scenarios
  • You plan to leverage Labor Management, advanced interleaving, or AI-driven optimization

Conclusion

The purpose is not to frame the decision as "old vs. new," but rather as "best fit for each site."

Organizations with diverse warehouse networks may benefit from using both solutions:

  • SAP Extended Warehouse Management for Private Cloud for high-throughput and complex distribution centers
  • SAP Logistics Management for smaller, less complex sites that still require structured, digital execution

Ultimately, the key question is not: Which solution is better? But rather: Where is each warehouse today—and where is it going?

entry.title: Resource Execution Constraints(REC) in SAP EWM - Practical Demo
entry.content:
In my earlier LinkedIn blog post, Configure REC in SAP EWM , I explained how to configure Resource Execution Constraints (REC) in SAP EWM.

In this blog post, I will focus on the testing behavior of REC using two practical scenarios.

Scenarios covered

  1. REC allows only one resource in an aisle at a time
    Only one resource can work in the aisle, regardless of resource type.

  2. REC allows multiple resource types
    One resource from each allowed resource type can work in the aisle at the same time.

System tested: S/4HANA 2023 FPS01

1. Bin master data

Maintain the REC Storage Group in the storage bin master for bins where resource restriction is required.

This REC Storage Group is linked to the REC Type.
The REC Type, together with the Resource Type, determines how many resources are allowed to work in that aisle or area.

Key point:
The bin-level REC Storage Group controls where the restriction applies.

2. Configuration overview

The main configuration involves the assignment of:

  • Resource Type
  • REC Type
  • Resource

The same REC Type can be assigned to multiple Resource Types.

For each REC Storage Group, you can control whether:

  • only one resource is allowed at a time, or
  • multiple resource types are allowed simultaneously

Example configuration behavior

  • If the REC rule is set to allow only one resource, then only one resource—regardless of type—can work in that aisle at a time.
  • If the REC rule is set to allow multiple resource types, then one resource from each permitted type can work in the aisle simultaneously.

3. Maintain velocity for resource types

Maintain the movement parameters for each resource type, for example:

  • Forklift
  • Reach truck

In my test, I defined two resource types:

  • RT10
  • RT11

For both resource types, I maintained:

  • Horizontal velocity
  • Vertical velocity

These values are important because SAP uses them to calculate travel time and movement duration.

4. Resource master setup

In the Resource Master, maintain the Default Storage Bin, which acts as the starting bin of the resource.

This is important because SAP uses it to calculate the travel distance.

Why this matters

If the default storage bin is not maintained, the RF transaction throws an error.

In my test, I assigned default storage bins for the following resources:

  • FL10
  • FL11

Scenario 1: Only one resource allowed in the aisle

In this scenario, the REC setup allows only one resource at a time, even if multiple resource types are assigned.

Step 1: Log in through RF

Navigate in RF using:

04 Outbound Processes -> 01 Picking -> 04 Picking by WO

Enter the Warehouse Order in the warehouse order field and press Enter.

Step 2: Check REC behavior

Once the warehouse order is assigned:

  • the Resource Table is updated with Entry Time and Exit Time
  • the Warehouse Order status changes to D - In Progress

This confirms that the aisle is now occupied by the first resource

Step 3: Test with another resource

Next, log in using another resource:

  • Resource: FL11
  • Resource Type: RT11

Follow the same RF path and enter a new warehouse order that points to the same aisle/bin area.

 

SAP throws an error message with the area already occupied.

Expected result

The second resource is not allowed to work in the same aisle.

You will notice:

  • the Resource Table is not updated

the second resource does not get access to the aisle under the REC rule

Additional observation

If the user backs out from the initial screen without confirming the warehouse order, the values in the Resource Table are cleared.

Pick by Queue behavior

For the same scenario, if you try Pick by Queue while one resource is already working in the aisle, SAP returns a message similar to:

“Suitable Warehouse Order does not exist.”

This is expected, because the aisle is already blocked by the active resource.

Travel path distance calculation

SAP also calculates the travel path distance based on the source and destination bins.

Example

  • Source Bin: GI-ZONE
  • Destination Bin: 0010-01-01-01

This can be checked in the Travel Path Evaluation settings in SAP Easy Access -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Settings

This helps to calculate total travel distance between the networks along with edges to move between the bin locations of two different storage types.

Scenario 2: REC allows multiple resource types

One resource from each allowed resource type can work in the aisle at the same time

In this scenario, I changed the REC configuration to allow multiple resource types.

Effectively, this means:

  • one resource from RT10 can work in the aisle
  • one resource from RT11 can also work in the same aisle at the same time

In my test, I maintained:

  • one resource for RT10
  • one resource for RT11

So, a total of two resources were allowed in the aisle simultaneously.

Step 1: Reprocess warehouse order

I tested the same process again using resource FL11, while FL10 was already active.

Expected result

This time:

  • the Resource Table is updated
  • both resources are allowed in the aisle
  • both warehouse orders move to D - In Progress

This confirms that the REC logic is now allowing one resource from each permitted resource type.

Key takeaway from both scenarios

The difference between the two scenarios is driven entirely by the REC configuration:

ScenarioBehavior
One-resource ruleOnly one resource can work in the aisle at a time
Multiple-resource-type ruleOne resource from each allowed resource type can work simultaneously

Enhancement option

If you need custom logic to calculate the leave time from the aisle, you can use the following BAdI: /SCWM/EX_RECGRP_LEAVETIME_CALC

This enhancement can be useful when the standard timing logic does not fully meet business requirements.

Conclusion

Resource Execution Constraints in SAP EWM are very useful for controlling aisle-level activity and avoiding congestion.

By combining:

  • REC Storage Group
  • REC Type
  • Resource Type
  • Resource Master settings
  • Travel path calculation

you can precisely control how many resources are allowed to operate in a specific area.

I hope this testing walkthrough helps you understand how REC behaves in different scenarios and how it can be used effectively in warehouse execution.

For help on this topic, refer to Resource Execution Constraints (REC) Using Radio Frequency | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: How to Configure Stock Removal Strategies in SAP EWM for Goods Issue Process
entry.content:
In SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM), stock removal strategies play a critical role in determining how the system selects stock during Goods Issue processes such as outbound deliveries, production supply, and stock transfers. However, before configuring or testing any stock removal strategy, it is essential to ensure that the required master data and warehouse settings are correctly maintained. Missing or incorrect prerequisite configurations can lead to unexpected stock determination results or process failures.

In this blog, we will walk through the essential prerequisite configurations required for stock removal strategies in SAP EWM. By the end of this guide, you will have a clear understanding of the foundational setup needed to ensure accurate and efficient stock selection during Goods Issue processing.

Step 1: Specify Stock Removal Rules

Config Path: SIMG/SPRO >> SCM Extended Warehouse Management >> Extended Warehouse Management >> Goods Issue Process >> Strategies >> Specify Stock Removal Rules

Stock Removal Rules in SAP EWM define the order in which stock (quants) is selected during warehouse task creation. They can be defined at the Storage Type Search Sequence, Storage Type Group, or Storage Type level.

The rules use configurable sorting criteria (ascending or descending) based on quant attributes and dynamic fields, enabling optimized stock selection according to business requirements.

Example: If you want to pick stock with an outdated alternative Unit of Measure (UoM) before other stock, configure the sorting based on the Preferred Unit of Measure field in ascending order. This ensures non-preferred UoM stock is picked first, helping reduce obsolete inventory

Step 2: Specify Storage Type Search Sequence

Config Path: SIMG/SPRO >> SCM Extended Warehouse Management >> Extended Warehouse Management >> Goods Issue Process >> Strategies >> Specify Storage Type Search Sequence

The Storage Type Search Sequence defines the order in which SAP EWM searches storage types for stock removal. It specifies the preferred storage types or storage type groups and the sequence in which the system searches them during warehouse task creation, ensuring efficient and optimized stock picking.

Step 3: Define Stock Removal Control Indicator

Config Path: SIMG/SPRO >> SCM Extended Warehouse Management >> Extended Warehouse Management >> Goods Issue Process >> Strategies >> Define Stock Removal Control Indicator

The Stock Removal Control Indicator allows you to control how products are picked during stock removal. It determines the preferred storage types or storage type groups for specific products and defines the stock sorting strategy, ensuring efficient and business-specific stock selection 

Step 4: Define Warehouse process Types

Config Path: SIMG/SPRO >> SCM Extended Warehouse Management >> Extended Warehouse Management >> Cross-Process Settings >> Warehouse Task >> Define Warehouse Process Type

Step 5: Determine Warehouse Process Type

Config Path: SIMG/SPRO >> SCM Extended Warehouse Management >> Extended Warehouse Management >> Cross-Process Settings >> Warehouse Task >> Determine Warehouse Process Type

This configuration enables SAP EWM to automatically determine the Warehouse Process Type (WPT) during warehouse request creation. The determined WPT controls how warehouse activities are executed and is also applied to special scenarios such as goods receipt with synchronous inventory posting, goods receipt from Manufacturing Execution Systems (MES), and field logistics processes.

Step 6: Determine Storage Type Search Sequence for Stock Removal

Config Path: SIMG/SPRO >> SCM Extended Warehouse Management >> Extended Warehouse Management >> Goods Issue Process >> Strategies >> Determine Storage Type Search Sequence for Stock Removal

The Storage Type Search defines how SAP EWM searches for stock during stock removal. It links the Storage Type Search Sequence with the Stock Removal Rule to determine the order of storage types and stock sorting. A preference list can also be used to prioritize specific storage types or storage type groups for stock removal.

Step 7: Assign Stock Removal Control in Product Master Data

 

 

Thanks,

Leelamohan Mannem

entry.title: SAP EWM Picking for Outbound Delivery: End-to-End Scenario Demonstration with Stock Removal Strategy
entry.content:
Introduction

After completing the prerequisite configuration for Stock Removal Strategies, the next step is to understand how these settings work in a real business scenario. In this blog, we will demonstrate the complete Picking process for an Outbound Delivery in SAP EWM and illustrate how the configured stock removal strategy determines the stock selected during Goods Issue processing.

If you haven't completed the prerequisite configuration yet, I recommend reading my previous blog first, where I covered the essential master data and configuration required before implementing Stock Removal Strategies. That foundational setup is a prerequisite for the scenario demonstrated in this blog.

Click Here - Pre-requisite Stock Removal Configuration Set Up in EWM

By the end of this guide, you'll have a clear understanding of how SAP EWM executes stock determination during picking and how the configured strategy influences the overall Goods Issue process.

 

MaterialWarehouseLocationQuantity
400312100AFS5 PC
Check Available stock in EWM (App ID: /scwm/mon)

Expand |--> Storage Bin |--> Physical Stock (Double Click)

Enter Product master number as 40031

A total of 70 PC of stock available

Step 1: Manage Sales order (App ID: F1873)

Enter Order Type and Organizational Data

Enter Sold-to-party, Ship-to-party, Customer reference and Customer ref. Date

Enter Item details and Quantity

Double click on Item number and review Pricing and shipping data details

Sales order #1256 has been created.

Step 2: Create Outbound Delivery (App ID: VL01N)

Navigate to outbound delivery from sales order screen:  Menu |--> Sales Document |--> Deliver

Enter Storage Location under picking tab

Outbound Delivery #80000471 has been created and distributed to WMS.

Step 3: Outbound Delivery Orders (App ID: /scwm/prdo)

Enter Outbound Delivery number and execute

Navigate to Warehouse task create screen: Menu |--> Follow-On Functions |--> Warehouse Task

Review the details by navigating tabs like (warehouse tasks, default values.. etc)

Once Create+Save clicked, automatic source bin will be picked based on the stock removal strategy defined (check introduction section for stock removal strategy blog link) 

Warehouse Order 203 Created.

Step 4: Check Outbound Delivery Orders (App ID: /scwm/mon)

Navigate to Outbound orders: Outbound |--> Documents |--> Outbound Delivery Orders (Double Click)

Select appropriate delivery item and click on warehouse task

Here, review the source and destination bin details and also other information

To confirm warehouse task in background, follow below shown path

Warehouse task 203 confirmed and stock moved to 9020 GI-ZONE.

Step 5: Post PGI in EWM system (App ID: /scwm/prdo)

Before PGI check Inventory Management stock in MMBE

Review, Picking completed and GI is yet to be posted

Once posted, you can review the GI status in same screen

Step 6: Check IM & EWM stock (App ID: MMBE & /scwm/mon)

IM Stock (5 PC reduced)

EWM Stock (5 PC reduced)

Step 7: Check Outbound Delivery Status (App ID: VL03N / F0867A)

Enter delivery document number, navigate to item details

Click on process Flow to check current status of delivery

Step 8: Create Billing Document (App ID: VF01)

same process flow can be reviewed in outbound delivery in VL03N

SAP S/4HANA Embedded EWM Putaway Strategy – Step-by-Step with Real Business Scenario

If you found this guide helpful, don't miss any other SAP MM WM blogs covering real-world automation, configurations tips, and best practices for procurement, inventory management, and warehouse management.

Click to view other SAP MM WM Blogs - SAP Community

 

Thanks,

Leelamohan Mannem

entry.title: SAP EWM Document Type Mapping Explained: ERP to EWM Integration Guide
entry.content:
Introduction

During inbound delivery integration, SAP EWM relies on document type mapping to correctly interpret and process documents received from SAP ERP or SAP S/4HANA. This configuration acts as a bridge between the two systems, ensuring seamless warehouse request creation, process control, and consistent document processing. In this blog, we'll explore the complete document type mapping configuration and understand its role in EWM integration.

Step 1: Document Types Check in ERP Inbound Delivery (App ID: VL33N)

Enter Inbound Delivery Number

review Item Category "ELN" and click on header details

review delivery type "EL"

Step 2: Document Types Check in EWM Inbound Delivery (App ID: /scwm/prdi)

Enter Inbound Delivery Number and execute 

Click on form view to view header details (Document Category & Document Type)

Similarly click on form view on Items to review (Item Category & Item Type)

Step 3: Define Document Type Mapping in ERP

Here ERP vs EWM header types have been mapped

Path: SIMG/SPRO >> SCM Extended Warehouse Management >> Extended Warehouse Management >> Interfaces >> ERP Integration >> Delivery Processing >> Map Document Types from ERP System to EWM

This activity maps document types between SAP ERP/SAP S/4HANA and SAP EWM warehouse request document types. It enables flexible process control by supporting one-to-one, one-to-many, and many-to-one document type mappings. The mapping is bidirectional, ensuring seamless document integration and processing between the enterprise management system and SAP EWM.

Here ERP vs EWM Item types have been mapped

Path: SIMG/SPRO >> SCM Extended Warehouse Management >> Extended Warehouse Management >> Interfaces >> ERP Integration >> Delivery Processing >> Map Item Types from ERP System to EWM

Step 4: Define Document Types for Inbound Delivery Process

Here the profiles against document type have been configured

Path: SIMG/SPRO >> SCM Extended Warehouse Management >> Extended Warehouse Management >> Goods Receipt Process >> Inbound Delivery >> Define Document Types for Inbound Delivery Process

This activity is used to define document types for the inbound delivery process. You can modify an existing document type, copy and adapt a standard document type, or create a new document type based on your business requirements.

ERP Ref. No is Doc No: This setting allows you to use the same document number for both the LE delivery and the corresponding warehouse request in SAP EWM. The ERP reference document number (REFDOCNO) is reused as the EWM document number (DOCNO), ensuring consistent document numbering across both systems.

Step 5: Define Item Types for Inbound Delivery Process

Path: SIMG/SPRO >> SCM Extended Warehouse Management >> Extended Warehouse Management >> Goods Receipt Process >> Inbound Delivery >> Define Item Types for Inbound Delivery Process

Step 6: Define Status Profiles

Check status profiles in inbound delivery item data

Path: SIMG/SPRO >> SCM Extended Warehouse Management >> Extended Warehouse Management >> Cross-Process Settings >> Delivery - Warehouse Request >> Status Management >> Define Status Profiles

This activity is used to define status profiles for delivery document and item types. Status profiles inherit status types from predefined system profiles, allowing you to exclude non-mandatory statuses based on business requirements. If no custom status profile is assigned, the standard system profile is applied automatically.

Select Status Profile and double click on Status Types

In similar manner, configure partner profiles

Path: SIMG/SPRO >> SCM Extended Warehouse Management >> Extended Warehouse Management >> Cross-Process Settings >> Delivery - Warehouse Request >> Partner Processing >> Define Partner Profiles

This activity is used to define partner profiles for delivery document and item types. Each partner profile is assigned to a system profile and inherits its partner role settings. You can create a custom partner profile to modify partner role assignments based on specific business requirements.

Step 7: Define Document Type Determination for Inbound Process

Path: SIMG/SPRO >> SCM Extended Warehouse Management >> Extended Warehouse Management >> Goods Receipt Process >> Inbound Delivery >> Define Document Type Determination for Inbound Delivery Process

In this IMG activity, you can specify the inbound values from which a document type can be derived. When a document is created, the system uses the these settings to automatically determine the corresponding document type.

 

Ref EWM blogs

SAP S/4HANA Embedded EWM Putaway Strategy – Step-by-Step with Real Business Scenario

SAP EWM Picking for Outbound Delivery: End-to-End Scenario Demonstration with Stock Removal Strategy

If you found this guide helpful, don't miss any other SAP MM WM blogs covering real-world automation, configurations tips, and best practices for procurement, inventory management, and warehouse management.

Click to view other SAP MM WM Blogs - SAP Community

 

Thanks,

Leelamohan Mannem

entry.title: Power up your SAP projects in Q3 with the SAP Enterprise Support Advisory Council!
entry.content:
Looking for more hands-on guidance and early access to the latest SAP Enterprise Support services? With SAP Enterprise Support, part of the Foundational Success Plan included in all cloud subscriptions, you already have a strong baseline to get started quickly, safeguard business continuity, and continuously realize value.

Through SAP Enterprise Support Advisory Council (ESAC) , you can go even further. As a cornerstone of this offering (and with no additional cost!), ESAC connects you directly with SAP experts and peers to bring practical, hands-on support to your projects, pilot new services ahead of general availability, and shape the future of SAP support.

Why join?

  • Get expert guidance for your projects
  • Access and influence new services before general release
  • Turn your feedback into real support improvements

Your commitment is minimal (only a few hours of engagement, depending on the workstream). In return, we simply ask for your honest feedback to help us continuously improve our services.

 What’s New in Q3?

entry.title: Cross Docking Is Not Speed — It Is Controlled Movement
entry.content:
Cross Docking in SAP EWM: Why Synchronization Matters More Than Speed

Tags: SAP Extended Warehouse Management, SAP Transportation Management

Cross Docking in SAP EWM Why Synchronization Matters More Than Speed

Introduction

Cross Docking is often described as a warehouse process that moves inbound goods directly to outbound delivery without long-term storage. While this definition is technically correct, it can also be misleading.

During my study of warehouse execution scenarios in SAP EWM, I began asking a different question:

Is Cross Docking really about moving goods faster, or is it about synchronizing warehouse and transportation activities?

In real warehouse operations, moving a pallet from one location to another is rarely the biggest challenge. The real challenge is ensuring that the correct material reaches the correct outbound shipment at the correct time while maintaining operational stability.

From this perspective, Cross Docking becomes less about speed and more about controlled synchronization.

The Common Misunderstanding

Cross Docking is commonly associated with three benefits:

  • Reduced storage time
  • Faster material movement
  • Lower inventory handling

These benefits are real, but they only exist when warehouse execution and transportation execution remain synchronized.

Consider the following situations:

  • The outbound vehicle arrives later than planned.
  • A staging area has reached capacity.
  • Labour resources become unavailable.
  • Transportation priorities change after inbound goods have already arrived.

In these scenarios, simply moving material faster does not improve warehouse performance. Instead, uncontrolled movement may create additional handling, staging congestion, or incorrect loading sequences.

The objective therefore shifts from fast execution to controlled execution.

A Railway Station Analogy

A busy railway station provides a useful analogy.

Passengers arrive on one train and transfer to another. At first glance, this resembles Cross Docking because people move directly from one transportation flow to another.

However, when delays occur, passengers are not simply pushed onto the next platform.

Instead:

  • Waiting areas are used.
  • Platforms are reassigned.
  • Passenger flow is redirected.
  • Station operators continuously adjust movement until connections become synchronized again.

The railway station is not optimizing walking speed.

It is optimizing coordinated movement.

Warehouse Cross Docking operates in a very similar way.

How This Translates into SAP EWM

In SAP EWM, Cross Docking is supported by execution processes that connect inbound logistics with outbound requirements.

Depending on the business scenario, SAP EWM may use:

  • Warehouse Tasks (WTs) to move goods directly toward outbound staging.
  • Storage Types and Staging Areas for temporary buffering when direct movement is not possible.
  • Warehouse Process Types (WPTs) to control execution logic.
  • Door determination and staging processes to ensure the correct loading sequence.
  • Warehouse Monitor visibility to identify execution bottlenecks.

The objective is not simply to reduce storage time.

The objective is to execute warehouse movement without disrupting outbound commitments.

The Role of SAP TM

Cross Docking becomes significantly more effective when integrated with SAP Transportation Management (SAP TM).

SAP TM provides information such as:

  • Planned transportation routes
  • Vehicle arrival times (ETA)
  • Transportation Units
  • Freight Orders
  • Delivery priorities

This information allows warehouse execution to align with transportation planning before physical loading begins.

For example:

If SAP TM indicates that a vehicle will arrive two hours later than originally planned, immediately moving Cross Docking inventory to the outbound door may actually increase warehouse congestion.

Instead, SAP EWM can temporarily stage the material until transportation execution becomes synchronized again.

In this situation, synchronization creates greater operational value than immediate movement.

Temporary Staging Is Not a Failure

One misconception is that successful Cross Docking always means zero staging.

Operationally, this is not always true.

Temporary staging may be the correct business decision when:

  • Transportation schedules change.
  • Dock capacity becomes constrained.
  • Loading priorities change.
  • Labour availability decreases.
  • Multiple outbound shipments compete for the same resources.

SAP EWM provides flexibility through staging areas and storage types that allow warehouses to absorb operational variation while maintaining execution control.

Temporary staging is therefore not necessarily inefficient.

In many situations, it prevents warehouse disruption.

Visibility Drives Synchronization

Successful Cross Docking depends on continuous operational visibility.

Warehouse teams must understand:

  • Which inbound deliveries have arrived.
  • Which outbound deliveries require immediate execution.
  • Which Transportation Units are delayed.
  • Which staging locations are approaching capacity.
  • Which warehouse resources are available.

Without visibility, warehouse execution becomes reactive.

With visibility, warehouse execution becomes synchronized.

Business Value

When Cross Docking is viewed only as a speed optimization technique, warehouse teams may focus exclusively on reducing storage time.

When viewed as a synchronization strategy, different objectives emerge:

  • Reduce unnecessary handling.
  • Protect outbound service levels.
  • Improve dock utilization.
  • Align warehouse execution with transportation planning.
  • Maintain operational control during disruptions.

These outcomes provide greater long-term business value than simply moving goods faster.

Conclusion

Cross Docking should not be viewed simply as direct material movement.

Its real value lies in synchronizing warehouse execution with transportation execution while maintaining operational control.

SAP EWM provides the execution capabilities required to manage warehouse movement.

SAP TM contributes transportation planning and visibility.

Together, these systems enable coordinated execution rather than isolated movement.

In my view, the most successful Cross Docking operations are not those that move the fastest.

They are the ones that remain synchronized even when business conditions change.

Author: Rahul Kumar
Manyat Thinking

"Fast movement creates activity. Synchronized movement creates business value."

entry.title: Updated Stay Certified Programs for TM and EWM
entry.content:
We are glad to announce the updates of our Stay Certified programs for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Editon, Extended Warehouse Management and Transportation Management. You can find the new assets (recorded live sessions) via the following links:

  • Stay Certified for EWM 
  • Stay Certified for TM 

What is Stay Certified?

Stay Certified is SAP's program for maintaining up-to-date certifications by completing periodic update learning modules and assessments designed for specific roles.

Which certifications are associated with this update?

  • The Stay Certified program for EWM is associated with the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, Extended Warehouse Management certification (C_S4EWM).
  • The Stay Certified program for TM is associated with the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, Transportation Management certification (C_S4TM).

How do I take the Stay Certified assessment?

To take the Stay Certified assessment, follow these steps:

  1. Log in to the learning.sap.com account.
  2. Click on "My Certifications" under your profile. You will then see a list of all your certifications.
  3.  Click on "Manage certification". 

 

  1. Click on the "Take Assessment" button.

Note: Access to the Stay Certified programs requires a subscription on the learning.sap.com platform.

entry.title: Decoupling Unload and GR with POSC setup where TU is used for SAP EWM Inbound operations
entry.content:
This Blog post is intended to provider reader  how to decouple Unload and GR with POSC setup where TU is used for Inbound operations 

Business Scenario –

For one of our Automotive customers the Yard operator uses their third-party application which does all the transportation planning and EWM is expected to follow the Check in , Movement to Parking lot/Door, unloading, check out at the TU level as per events received from Third party. All the physical movements done with third party applications had to be in sync with TU status and business should be able to track exact TU status for Check in, Dock to door, Unloading etc. in SAP.

There was an ask to Decouple the Unloading and GR in SAP where-in we had Complex Unloading setup with steps for Unloading, Deconsolidate and Put away.

As per standard SAP setup, the Unloading Warehouse task Confirmation post Implicit Good receipt in the background however business ask was to decouple the same behavior.

We could achieve this setup by discarding Unloading WT creation step in the POSC and changing the Goods movement setup in SAP to Delivery level. Please go through the below details to understand current behavior and solution followed.

Current setup Behavior -

As per the screen capture, the TU is docked to the door and waiting for Unloading to be done. 

SAP copies Warehouse door from TU to IBD and same can be seen in the below screen shot. As per these values ,  System creates Unloading task from TU to ‘Staging Bay’

Next when We get event from third party Application that Unloading is finished, EWM Unloading task execution happens in the background and TU status gets updated with Unloading start/Finish. Once we set this Unloading finish at the TU system confirms unloading task in the background and simultaneously posts GR as well.

As per below screenshot , the unloading task is confirmed when we select ‘ Unload finished’ at the TU level and 2nd warehouse is created to move Nested HU with 2 products to Deconsolidation area.

Nested HU with 2 different products.

 

New Setup Behaviour-

Firstly, Configuration for Goods movement control must be set to ‘1. Goods movement to Storage Bin Specified in Delivery’.

New POSC is created with steps for Deconsolidation and Put away (Deleted Step IB01 for unloading)

Removed door from Staging area and door determination otherwise Goods movement bin is copied as door bin in the replicated IBD in EWM.

The Door bin is removed from  assignment of Door to SCU and storage bin.

Now as we can see in the Screen shot below, Staging bin is copied as ‘ Goods movement bin’

Later when TU is docked to door, the Door is copied from TU to IBD, but Goods movement bin is not updated and remains ‘ Staging bin’

As per TU status, Arrival at check point and docking to door is finished and now TU is ready to execute unloading.

As per our setup, we get unloading start and finish events from third part at this point of time and we set TU status accordingly.

To simulate above posting, execution is done in the RF as below.

Execution by RFUI menu--> Rec HU by ASN

 

 

As we can see the unloading , GR and WT creation status are all blanks.

Yard operator selects push button – ‘ UnLo’ and Unloading status is set to done.

The Unloading status is reflected in the in TU and most importantly the GR status remains open.

IBD shows ‘Unloading’ Completed but ‘Good receipt’ not started.

Next truck will move back to check point. TU departs from warehouse, and TU status is set to completed.

Now Logistic operators can do GR and rest of the warehouse operations at their own convenience . System’s only prerequisite to execute other warehouse operations with RF is to finish Unloading which is already done.

To execute GR, Logistic operator selects same RF menu -->Rec HU by ASN.

Unloading is already done so he will select GR and WT to move the pallet to Deconsolidation is created in the background.

 

Good receipt status is set to complete and Only Deconsolidation warehouse task is created to move the Pallet from Staging bin to Deconsolidation workstation. Unloading tasks are not created as per changed setup.

Conclusion –

Decoupling Unload and GR with POSC setup where TU is used for Inbound operations can be achieved by disregarding Unloading warehouse steps from POSC and setting up goods movement to TU.

This approach enables us to handle Missing pallets/extra pallets in the IBD with very ease for warehouse employees. Reversing GR and ‘reverse unloading ( without WT ) can be executed at IBD level.

Thanks for reading the blog, I hope that this can be beneficial.

Thank you,

Harish Patil

entry.title: No More Workarounds at the Receiving Dock
entry.content:
A Quick Recap: ASR Unloading on the RF Device

In the Advanced Shipping and Receiving scenario, the inbound process follows a well-defined sequence. Once a freight order is created and the truck is checked in at the receiving point, the warehouse worker takes over at the RF device to execute the physical unloading — scanning each handling unit (HU) as it comes off the truck and confirming it against the expected freight order content.

This handshake between TM and EWM is what makes ASR powerful: the freight order drives the warehouse execution, status updates flow both ways, and everything stays in sync. The warehouse monitor, the Fiori app "Load or Unload Freight Orders," and the RF UI all reflect the same picture.

Before FPS1 2025, the ASR RF unloading transaction offered no structured exception path when an HU was missing or when stock arrived in an unexpected packing format. Warehouse workers had to exit the RF flow, escalate to the office, and fix the discrepancy manually — in a transaction that was never designed for that. For high-throughput warehouses running on lean staffing, that is a costly interruption.

What FPS1 2025 Delivers: HUMI and CRHU

SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS1 introduces two new capabilities in the ASR RF unloading flow, delivered under roadmap item INNO_64055 (application component TM-FRM-ASR-LUF):

Exception Code HUMI — "This HU is not here"

HUMI is a new exception code available in the ASR RF UI during unloading. It is triggered when a handling unit that is registered in the freight order — and therefore expected to arrive — is physically absent from the truck.

With HUMI, the worker can now flag the exception directly on the RF device. The system registers that the specific HU was expected but not received, and the freight order and inbound delivery reflect that status. The process can continue for the HUs that are present — no full stop, no manual cleanup session after the fact.

Practical scenario: A truck from a regular supplier arrives with 11 of 12 expected pallets. The 12th pallet was left behind at the origin warehouse. The RF worker scans the 11 pallets, reaches the missing one, triggers HUMI, and the unloading process closes cleanly for what was received. The freight order records the discrepancy. The procurement and logistics teams see the shortfall immediately — without anyone needing to call the supervisor over.

 Function Button CRHU — "This stock is packed, but the system does not know it"

CRHU (Create HU) is a new function button in the ASR RF unloading flow that addresses a mirror-image problem. Here, the system records items as loose, unpacked stock — but physically, the delivery arrives on pallets or in cartons with an HU that does not yet exist in EWM.

When stock originates from a supplier that does not transmit HU-level packing data, EWM holds the delivery items at the item level with no associated handling units. The physical delivery, however, might be fully palletized. Without CRHU, the only way to reconcile this was a post-unloading HU creation in a desktop transaction — a step that pulled people off the floor.

With CRHU available directly on the RF device, the worker can create the handling unit on the spot, associate it with the correct delivery items, and keep the physical flow and the system in sync — all without leaving the RF transaction.

Practical scenario: A supplier ships 200 units. The ASN contains item-level data but no packing information. EWM expects 200 individual pieces. The truck arrives with 10 pallets of 20 units each. The RF worker triggers CRHU for each pallet, creates the HUs directly on screen, and the stock is received with the correct packing structure from the start — no rework in the putaway zone needed.

 Why This Matters for ASR Deployments Specifically

In non-ASR inbound flows, EWM has had exception handling mechanisms for some time. The gap was specifically in the ASR context — where the freight order from TM drives the RF unloading flow — and this is where FPS1 2025 closes it.

For customers running the Advanced TM-EWM Inbound ASR process (scope item 7VE), these additions directly improve one of the most operationally sensitive steps in the end-to-end flow. The key benefits confirmed by SAP for INNO_64055 are:

  • Improved process flow for warehouse workers during unloading — the RF flow no longer has a dead end when reality diverges from the plan.
  • Reduced clerical work — discrepancies are handled at the point of occurrence, not discovered and corrected in a separate back-office session.

 Availability and Configuration

 

Parameter

 

Detail

 

Available since

 

SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS1 (Q1 2026)

 

Deployment models

 

SAP S/4HANA on-premise  |  SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition

 

Application component

 

TM-FRM-ASR-LUF (Loading and Unloading Freight Orders)

 

Activation required

 

No separate licensing activation — available with ASR RF unloading

 

Roadmap innovation ID

 

INNO_64055

 

SAP Help Portal

 

https://help.sap.com/whats-new/5fc51e30e2744f168642e26e0c1d9be1

 What else is new for ASR in FPS1 2025?

HUMI and CRHU are not the only ASR enhancements in FPS1 2025. The same release also delivers:

BAdIs for prechecks before loading execution (INNO_64044)

Customer-specific validation logic invoked before loading starts — in the RF screen, the Fiori "Load or Unload Freight Orders" app, and the EWM desktop transaction /SCWM/LOAD. A clean, supported extensibility point for business-specific loading conditions.

Other process and UX updates (INNO_64046)

Enhanced freight-order search in wave maintenance (/SCWM/WAVE) and the warehouse monitor (/SCWM/MON); weight and volume correction for unpacked items in outbound delivery orders; synchronization of actual EWM weight/volume back into TM documents.

Decentral integration with SAP GTS (INNO_60203)

Full SAP Global Trade Services integration for the side-by-side TM+EWM ASR scenario: compliance checks, customs declarations, unloading permission requests, and transit closure for both inbound and outbound flows.

The Bottom Line

If your team has been working around ASR unloading limitations — coaching RF workers through manual exception procedures, running post-unload cleanup sessions, or accepting stock accuracy gaps because the RF flow had no graceful exit — FPS1 2025 is worth a close look.

HUMI and CRHU are not glamorous features. They will not make the headline in any conference keynote. But in the warehouses where ASR is live, they will quietly eliminate two of the most common sources of friction in the inbound receiving workflow. 
That is exactly the kind of improvement that makes a real difference on the floor.

 References:

SAP Help Portal — What's New, Supply Chain > Warehousing:
https://help.sap.com/whats-new/5fc51e30e2744f168642e26e0c1d9be1?Line_of_Business=Supply+Chain&Business_Area=Warehousing

SAP Roadmap Explorer — INNO_64055 (Q1 2026):
https://roadmaps.sap.com/board?range=CURRENT-LAST#Q1%202026;INNO=87D39AF950211EDFAFA3AE22EF085974
